标题: Cisco in Talks to Buy AI Security Startup Astrix for at Least $250 Million
内容:
Cisco Systems is in talks to acquire Astrix Security, a five-year-old Israeli cybersecurity startup that sells software to monitor and secure AI agents, for between $250 million and $350 million, according to people with knowledge of the deal. That would represent at least a 25% premium to the startup’s last valuation of around $200 million, one of the people said.
The deal talks come as the popularity of AI agents—AI software that can take actions on behalf of users on their devices—has exploded in recent months. Concerns over the risk these agents pose to companies have led large software vendors to introduce new features that protect clients against this risk or to buy startups with these offerings.
The risk of agents going rogue “is going to be a huge problem,” said Cisco President Jeetu Patel on The Information’s
TITV streaming show late last month
, after the company announced updates to its software to protect against vulnerabilities stemming from AI agents. “You have to delegate work to these agents and the difference between delegation and trusted delegation is the difference … between bankruptcy and market leadership.”
Spokespeople for Cisco and Astrix didn’t respond to requests for comment. The discussions are ongoing and the companies may not reach an agreement.
Cisco, which sells networking equipment used in data centers, has for years been trying to grow its sales of subscription-based software, including cybersecurity tools. In recent years, the company has been expanding its offerings that protect against AI threats. Earlier this week, it agreed to acquire Galileo Technologies, a startup that develops tools to monitor and evaluate AI models and agents. Two years ago it bought Robust Intelligence, which sells software meant to help companies prevent security vulnerabilities in their AI applications.
Cybersecurity concerns have risen over the last year with the releases of advanced AI models like Anthropic’s Claude Mythos, which the company says it’s
testing with an early group of partners
due to the model’s ability to exploit security vulnerabilities. Separately, the rising use of AI agents within enterprises
has put cybersecurity executives on edge
given that, in some cases, these agents have wreaked havoc on
company systems
and
users’ devices
, including by triggering a security alert at Meta Platforms and mass deleting emails.
Those worries have made startups focused on safeguarding companies from AI risk into acquisition targets. ServiceNow in December said
it would buy Veza
, a five-year-old startup whose software helps companies see which of their employees and AI agents have access to customer data. And Palo Alto Networks
last spring acquired Protect AI
, another startup that aims to safeguard companies’ AI applications.
Tel Aviv-based Astrix has raised $85 million in funding from investors including Menlo Ventures, CRV and Bessemer Venture Partners.
If completed, the deal would be one of the first exits from a joint fund backed by Anthropic and Menlo Ventures that was formed in July 2024 to back AI startups. The $100 million Anthology Fund has invested in more than 45 companies, including expert network Mercor, AI interpretability startup Goodfire and AI math startup Axiom.
​​Other startups also aim to tackle AI agent risks. Oasis Security, a startup that competes with Astrix, recently raised $120 million from Sequoia Capital and Accel.

标题: How a Self-Taught Programmer Became the Father of Claude Code
内容:
Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, almost parted ways with Anthropic for good last summer. Instead, he has helped shape the coding agent into a megahit that has closed the distance with rival OpenAI.
Claude Code has supercharged Anthropic’s growth. After rising from $1 billion in December to $2.5 billion in February, Claude Code’s annualized revenue has accelerated in recent weeks, Cherny said, helping
propel Anthropic’s revenue on that basis to $30 billion
.
The growth vindicates Cherny’s decision to return to Anthropic after making a brief sojourn last summer to coding-tool startup Cursor that at the time seemed to give that smaller rival a major boost. In the months since Cherny came back, Claude Code has shaken up a tech industry that was already enthralled by AI, spawning a new era of AI agents, hastening a decline in software stocks and remaking the jobs of software engineers.
It has all made for a dizzying year for Cherny, 34, a self-taught programmer who quit his undergraduate studies at the University of California, San Diego, before finishing his economics degree and founding a startup. He’s focusing now on adding new features to make Claude Code easier to use as the AI models powering it continue to advance. A year from now, “we’re going to see a world where anyone can do software engineering,” he said in an interview. The same way “coding is solved for me, it’s going to be solved for everyone.”
Cherny and his team are now working to extend Claude Code’s automation of software engineering into other kinds of work, such as legal and financial tasks, through its offshoot, Claude Cowork. Anthropic’s latest model, Mythos, has already scored some
blockbuster coding results
, and early testers said Mythos was the first model that could handle hourslong agentic coding tasks,
according to Anthropic
. Due to its capacity for cyberattacks, Anthropic is refraining from releasing the model for now but says it
aims to add
a comparably capable model for Claude Code in the future.
Claude Code also faces intensifying competition from OpenAI, which is racing to strengthen its own AI coding product, Codex. And Anthropic hit a setback late last month when it accidentally released the software behind Claude Code that allows its models to act on their own. That has offered competitors a rare look at how Claude Code works under the hood, along with
planned updates
that would allow it to act more proactively and visualize Claude Code as a virtual duck avatar.
The challenges to keeping Claude Code’s edge are daunting, but its current success also seemed far-fetched a year ago.
Cherny joined Anthropic in September 2024 after nearly seven years at Meta Platforms, where he helped lead efforts to improve the quality of Meta’s code. He said he is drawn to Anthropic’s mission of developing advanced AI for the benefit of humanity. He became the first engineer on Claude Code, which was released in February 2025—and flopped.
“It didn’t just skyrocket immediately.” Cherny said. It “plateaued for a while.” The product only took off after smarter models landed to power it.
Last May, sitting backstage at Anthropic’s first developer conference, Cherny had a bit of a eureka moment about its coding abilities. Perched among the stage crew and lighting operators, he was testing Claude Code with the new models Anthropic had released that day, Opus 4 and Sonnet 4.
“I just remember feeling like, ‘Holy shit, this thing. Like, it works,’” Cherny said. As a result of its newfound powers, Claude went from generating about 10% of his code to closer to 30%, he said.
Still, there were other moments of doubt before Claude Code really took off. In July last year, Cherny and Catherine Wu, the product lead for Claude Code, departed to Cursor, which had gained huge buzz for its AI coding tool. Both were given more senior roles there, Cherny as the company’s chief architect and head of engineering, and Wu as head of product.
Cursor is both a customer of Anthropic, whose models it uses, and a competitor for coding customers, and the industry saw the duo’s move as a coup for the smaller company. Cherny said in an email that he was drawn to the people at Cursor but quickly realized it was a mistake to leave Anthropic and reached out about returning. Within a couple weeks, both he and Wu were back.
“Coming back was 100% about missing the mission. There is literally nothing that I could think of doing that feels more urgent and important than what I am doing now, except maybe working on alignment research directly,” Cherny said, referring to efforts to get AI models’ goals to match those of their developers. Since coming back, he has continued to lead Claude Code’s engineering work and shape which features the team plans to add to the product. He and Wu, along with others on the team, report to Fiona Fung, who manages the Code and Cowork operation.
Buoyed by Claude Code’s success, Anthropic’s annualized revenue
recently surpassed
OpenAI’s. Claude Code allowed Anthropic to gain on OpenAI because it equipped Anthropic’s coding-savvy models with software for working autonomously across several files. Last November, it got another big boost with Anthropic’s release of Claude Opus 4.5.
That was when AI reached human-level performance at software engineering, said Tian Yu, a venture investor at Micron Ventures who uses Claude Code. He said the new model allowed Claude Code to run for longer, reason more effectively and write code with fewer bugs.
Anthropic co-founder and CEO Dario Amodei.
(Getty Images)
Yu, who quickly found himself staying up until 2 a.m. to code with Claude, used it so much that he started running into Anthropic’s usage limits. He canceled his $200 per month ChatGPT subscription and subscribed to Anthropic instead.
“My Claude Code is like an intern trapped in my computer, and it’s actually faster than most developers I know,” he said.
Many techies didn’t try out Claude Code until they were on winter break in December, with more spare time. Even within Anthropic, employees returned after vacation saying: “Oh my god, I almost have an existential crisis—this thing is just so good at coding,” Cherny recalled.
Claude Code’s abilities to edit across different files and translate code from one programming language to another have won over big customers, including Netflix, Accenture and Uber, according to Anthropic. The software engineering market it is going after is worth over $1 trillion, Cherny said.
The arrival of Claude Code and its agentic ilk has fueled uncertainty over the future of legacy software businesses, slashing share prices for companies such as Salesforce, ServiceNow and Workday. It also has fueled worries that coding agents could put software engineers out of work.
That’s ironic given that Cherny learned to program when he was in middle school, writing web code for listings on eBay and programming graphing calculators. Though Claude Code has freed many software engineers from manual coding, Cherny said he believes programming remains “an intensely practical skill.”
Cherny acts as a sort of public ambassador for Claude Code. Early this month, he presented the news on social media that subscribers could no longer use Claude Code in third-party software such as OpenClaw, the viral software for creating personal agents.
He spent the next 45 minutes answering questions on X from outraged OpenClaw users, then resumed after 11 p.m. Even OpenClaw creator Peter Steinberger
appreciated
Cherny’s contributions to OpenClaw. “While I think what Anthropic does is sad for the ecosystem,” he wrote, “I wanna give Boris credit for doing what he can to soften the fallout.”
Cherny said in an interview he hasn’t tried OpenClaw himself. But Claude Code has recently
drawn inspiration
for many new features from OpenClaw, such as the ability to put Claude to work from a user’s phone.
Claude Code led to a wave of programmers deleting the apps they were using to read and write code—a blow to Cursor, the previous king of AI coding. “I’ve given up on Cursor,” said Rob Sun, founding engineer at Dexterity, which makes industrial robots for unloading trucks. Claude Code has helped him with work including a computer vision project that took it only a couple days to complete.
“It’s a set of problems that I think I never would have solved myself,” Sun said. “I would have needed a team of 10 to 30 research engineers and a year, maybe a couple months, to do it. And if it was just myself, it would probably take three years of uninterrupted time.”
A Cursor spokesperson said it “is still rapidly growing, specifically in large businesses and enterprises.” The company has revamped Cursor’s interface to center coding agents
from multiple providers
, including Cursor’s own agents, as well as Claude Code and Codex. The new interface also preserves the option for users to inspect their code line by line.
OpenAI, which recently axed its AI video app, Sora, to prioritize business-focused AI products, has redoubled its efforts on its coding agent, which has won over many software developers, who say that it can be
more efficient
than Claude Code. Some are using Claude Code and Codex together.
These days, Cherny said, Claude Code writes all of his code. The team used Claude Code to create the final version of Claude Cowork—in just 10 days. The product continues to self-improve: It automatically pulls in user feedback from across the internet and fixes its own bugs, Cherny said.
The agent’s abilities have enabled the engineering team that built it to stay small, said Cherny, who estimates they have the highest revenue per engineer in the world, though he declined to share the size of the revenue or the team.
For example, Cherny’s team members have to fill out a spreadsheet once a week to provide a status update on their work. Cherny has Cowork look over the spreadsheet and message a reminder on Slack to anyone who hasn’t filled it out. “I just go get coffee while it does,” he said.
Cherny also is trying out Claude for booking flights and paying parking tickets.
Last month, he was visiting friends in Seattle who wanted to go clamming. Cherny struggled to navigate the website for the local fish and wildlife department to request a license, so he appealed to Cowork, which figured out the website, asked Cherny for his credit card, paid $7 and got the right license.

标题: SK Hynix Is the Overlooked Memory Chip Maker
内容:
The memory chip market right now is governed by a triumvirate of companies, led by South Korean firm SK Hynix, followed by Samsung, also of South Korea, and
U.S. chipmaker Micron
. The three stocks soared more than 200% over the last year—and yet they’re still cheap, especially relative to Nvidia’s stock.
Investors aren’t giving the companies credit for their stunning growth. SK Hynix and Micron each increased sales nearly 50% in 2025 and analysts expect the companies to grow sales 159% and 191%, respectively, in 2026, according to Koyfin, far more than the 71% growth Nvidia is expected to deliver in its next fiscal year, which ends in January. Samsung’s business is far more diversified, making its performance less indicative of growing memory demand.
What gives? Investors know that the business of memory chip makers has been cyclical in the past, depending on demand for memory flowing from PC and smartphone makers, formerly the main consumers. During downturns, revenue plunged. As a result, investors have been wary of overpaying for the stocks.
But that history is less relevant now. The memory gear the three companies make is crucial for AI chips, demand for which is off the charts. That has created a shortage expected to last at least into 2028, UBS said in a report this week. Even better, this memory is valuable for any kind of AI chips, not just those made by Nvidia. It’s vital for those Broadcom designed for Google, as well as newer chips designed for AI inference, the process by which models respond to queries.
Moreover, the companies are signing longer-term contracts and selling to a broader set of customers than in the past, said Rob Wilson, a portfolio manager at Baillie Gifford who invests primarily in non-U.S. companies. Those changes argue for their getting a higher valuation than in the past.
“Historically, these companies have been in the investor sin bin. People have not liked them on the basis of them being viciously cyclical, and they haven’t been very disciplined further back in the past,” said Wilson, who has been buying SK Hynix’s shares recently. He was initially hesitant to get involved with the stock after an early-2000s price-fixing scandal that implicated all three memory makers. “But the nature of their business is totally transformed,” Wilson said.
And yet these stocks still trade at an enormous discount to Nvidia. For instance, SK Hynix and Micron are trading at under 5 times forward earnings, while Nvidia is at 22 times. On a multiple of last year’s book value, which analysts use for the sector, SK Hynix trades at 6 times, while Micron is at 6.5 times. Nvidia is valued much more richly, at 33 times.
The Right Premium
What’s the right valuation for the memory chip stocks? Wilson has both Nvidia and SK Hynix in his portfolio and argues both are undervalued at current levels, but he still believes Nvidia deserves something of a premium because it is the only supplier of complete systems for AI data centers. And unlike the memory chip makers, Nvidia outsources its manufacturing, which has in the past supported its comparatively higher margins.
But Wall Street analysts think Nvidia’s premium is too big. The gap in valuation between the memory makers and Nvidia “will close,” said John Vinh, an equity analyst covering semiconductor firms at KeyBanc Capital Markets. “The question is, how much will it close? I think a lot of it has to do with whether Micron and the other memory guys can get long-term agreements in place” with their customers.
All three memory chip manufacturers have talked up their efforts to sign long-term deals. Vinh says such deals come with two benefits—locking in a minimum price for a product and compelling customers to pay for capacity in advance at the start of each year.
Sentiment is shifting toward the memory chip makers. Joe Tigay, a portfolio manager at investment advisory firm Equity Armor Investments, has been trimming down his large position in Nvidia recently and buying up stock in Micron. While he’s still bullish on Nvidia, Tigay said he has been adjusting his allocation out of a desire to “diversify out of the major leaders in the AI space and say there’s going to be more winners than just that.”
One factor that caused the memory stocks to stumble last month came in the form of a press release from Google unveiling a new algorithm that could run with significantly less memory. Investors panicked, assuming that would hurt the memory manufacturers. But Wilson, from Baillie Gifford, thinks this could actually benefit them.
“To me, it seems relatively obvious to me that there will be ways to use more memory as a result of optimizations that happen downstream of the memory,” Wilson said.
Why SK Hynix Stands Out
Of the three, SK Hynix stands out as the stock with the best potential. Its gross margin of 60% and operating margin of 49% last year surpassed Micron’s respective margins. What’s more, Wall Street analysts expect its gross margin to surge this year, surpassing their projections of Nvidia’s over a similar period.
Another catalyst for the stock is that SK Hynix intends to list its shares in the U.S. later this year, its CEO told investors during its annual shareholder meeting last month. It plans to raise $14 billion in the listing to fund new chipmaking factories in Indiana as well as in South Korea, Reuters
reported
.
The company is set to report its first-quarter earnings later this month, Analysts polled by Koyfin are predicting it will increase its revenue 166% to roughly $32 billion. They also expect its gross profit margin to rise to 75.8%, up from 69% in the previous quarter.
Analysts will be looking for any updates on the status of new deals. For instance, Korean news outlets
reported
this week that SK Hynix was in the midst of negotiating long-term contracts to supply memory to Microsoft and Google.
Wilson says he will be paying close attention to any disclosures the company provides around the terms of such long-term customer contracts, many of which it is currently negotiating.
Ultimately, he’s optimistic that strong demand for memory, particularly from large AI customers, can buoy the stock for the next few years—as long as the big tech firms keep up their lavish capital expenditures, something he sees as likely.
“The end-demand customers are going parabolic in terms of their revenue profile and aren’t pulling back at all,” Wilson said. “They’re all compute constrained in terms of their ability to build revenue. The more compute they get, the more memory they’ll get, the more revenue they can generate.”

标题: Silicon Valley, Ready to Laugh Again?
内容:
Welcome, Weekenders!
In this newsletter:
•
The Big Read:
Meet
the father
of Claude Code
•
Artificial Intelligence:
The
hijinks
around tokenmaxxing
•
Plus, Recommendations—our weekly pop culture picks:
“
Who Blew Up the Georgia Guidestones?
” “
Those Who Are About to Die
” and “
It Was Just an Accident
”
Over a good many years
, “Silicon Valley,” the HBO show, accomplished a very rare feat: It got the elite of Silicon Valley, the place, to laugh at themselves and take themselves a little less seriously, an achievement as difficult as explaining, say, quantum computing. It’s no easy task to win over the set you’re lampooning, making the series’s tech land popularity a real testament to showrunner Mike Judge, who, beneath the gags, often presented techies as bleakly as “Veep” portrayed our nation’s politicos.
As tech has gotten zanier lately, a pressure campaign among fans has mounted for Judge to reboot his show. He has
flatly refused
. On Sunday evening, a new AMC show, “The Audacity,” hopes to begin occupying the cultural space Judge has given up.
The show is dark, funny and detailed, much as you’d expect from its creator, Jonathan Glatzer, a former “Succession” writer, who enjoys skewering tech mogulness as much as “Succession” delighted in aping Murdochian antics.
Where “Silicon Valley” had Pied Piper and “Succession” had Waystar Royco, “The Audacity” has Hypergnosis, a startup run by CEO Duncan Park (Billy Magnussen). He has just fumbled a sale of his startup to a bigger competitor, Cupertino (yes, really), and faces any number of consequences for it.
Duncan is someone who has thrived through ample self-confidence and a nice smile, but bless him, he’s dim, managing tries at software-is-eating-the-world grand thoughts with the delicacy of Yogi Berra. (In one heated exchange, he confuses Nostradamus and Nosferatu.) Unsurprisingly, he’s in therapy, but by the end of the pilot, he has used an all-powerful, data-guzzling AI to blackmail his counselor (Sarah Goldberg) into becoming a co-conspirator. Duncan’s domestic life is a mess, too, as he and his wife, Lili (Lucy Punch), trade emotional barrages through their pursuit of an open marriage. As these adults scamper and scheme, their children are collateral damage, warped both by their parents’ poor parenting and, of course, the tech products that underpin the prosperity around them.
“I think there are attributes to the Valley that deserve interrogation,” Glatzer said, following a screening of the show I attended earlier this week. “You know that move-fast-and-break-things mentality—a bull-in-a-china-shop metaphor—has become something positive, when in fact it’s like, ‘No, there’s gonna be a lot of broken dishes.’”
Like “Succession,” “The Audacity” has a high-gloss verisimilitude, which is part of the satire. Many of the interiors resemble an OpenAI demo video; I noticed an expensive-looking revolving door that I swear I’ve seen before in a Hillsborough mansion. To burnish the authenticity, Glatzer took as many Bay Area meetings as he could get. Mostly, he met a warm reception—with a notable exception. “Nobody from Apple would talk with me,” he recalled. “It’s probably why Cupertino is mentioned [in the show] so many times.”
While Silicon Valley is littered with the detritus of failed startups, Hollywood back lots are piled up with the remains of shows about tech and startups that never quite took off. Netflix tried one with Rob Lowe in 2023, “Unstable,” for instance, and even though it was very funny, it flopped. Likewise, the more somber “Devs” from director Alex Garland went nowhere on FX. And AMC had only limited luck getting people interested in “Halt and Catch Fire,” a tale about the 1980s PC revolution.
Glatzer hopes he has made Duncan’s graspingness recognizable enough to win over a wide audience—from within Silicon Valley and outside it, too.
“He’s a wannabe titan, and I think that gives him a desperation—a desire to climb up the rungs of the ladder—that makes him relatable,” he said. “He’s not a billionaire. We discussed this, and we estimated that he’s worth just $500 million.”
What else from this week…
Speaking of an all-powerful AI, Anthropic’s Mythos model has
freaked out
a lot
of
people
, including
the Federal Reserve and the Treasury Department
.
In California, the Salton Sea is an environmental disaster. The lake is also, as Gov. Gavin Newsom termed it,
something akin
to “the Saudi Arabia of lithium.”
The half-million-dollar, “Survivor”-themed outing in Honduras thrown by Plex, a streaming video startup, may be
the Fyre Festival of corporate retreats
.
Between
the death of Sora
and Ben Affleck
selling his AI startup
to Netflix for possibly as much as $600 million, I’d wager that the sentiment on AI will shift pretty noticeably in Hollywood this year. I suspect we’ll hear more filmmakers such as Steven Soderbergh express sentiments
like this one
: “AI has been helpful in creating thematically surreal images that occupy a dream space rather than a literal space. And that’s been really fun because you need a Ph.D. in literature to tell it what to do.”
The New York Times
appended a pointed editor’s note
to that viral story about a two-person health tech that used AI to zoom off to $1.8 billion in sales.
Want to stop anesthetizing your kids with a smartphone when you’re out to dinner? A
more pertinent shopping guide
may never have existed.
Tax day is nigh, and accountants
have no idea
what to do with their clients’ prediction market spoils.
—Abram Brown (
abe@theinformation.com
) .
Weekend’s Latest Stories
The Big Read
How a Self-Taught Programmer Became the Father of Claude Code
Anthropic’s Boris Cherny has given the AI startup
its breakthrough hit
, with its revenue continuing to skyrocket past $2.5 billion.
Artificial Intelligence
The ‘Tokenmaxxing’ Tide May Already Be Turning
The practice of maximizing token usage may have caught on with employees at Meta and elsewhere. But companies in most of the business world
are developing
other ways of encouraging AI use among staff.
Listening:
“
Who Blew Up the Georgia Guidestones?
”
“Some people are convinced that aliens built the Guidestones,” explains Tyler McBrien, a journalist and Georgia native long fascinated by the pair of Stonehenge-esque granite pillars that towered over a stretch of the state near its South Carolina border. “Other people
just
know
that the lizard people were behind it.”
For more than two decades, the Guidestones’ central riddle was their creator’s identity: They’d been commissioned in secret, then erected by a local masonry outfit. In 2022, the mystery deepened further when someone destroyed them. McBrien tries to unravel both ends of the puzzle, and in the process, he ends up presenting a picture of the fears, passions and obsessions that drew people to the enigmatic monument.
—Abram Brown
Reading:
“
Those Who Are About to Die
” by Harry Sidebottom
Think days have gotten long in this era of 996 and AI FOMO? Eh, you’ve got it easy! A day as a gladiator in Ancient Rome’s fighting pits—now
that
was a test of workplace survival. In “Those Who Are About to Die,” Harry Sidebottom, a novelist and Oxford University don, structures his dry-humored history of Roman gladiators based on the 24 hours that typically surrounded a match, beginning with the celebratory supper the previous evening and carrying through to the stadium procession and final showdown. It is a keen-eyed study of a small segment of Rome’s population, which in turn reflects much about how that grand civilization functioned as a whole, particularly the relationship between the poor and the powerful. And Sidebottom’s details are memorable.
Contrary to popular depiction, gladiators resemble nothing like
Kirk Douglas
—or even
Russell Crowe
. They were pudgy, and for good reason: Their carb-heavy diets—a lot of barley and beans—meant a “thick layer of subcutaneous fat that shielded vital organs,” Sidebottom writes. “This enabled the combatants to take flesh wounds that would bleed profusely but would not prevent them from continuing to fight. Fat gladiators made a better visual spectacle.”
A gladiatorial career tended to last around three years, and combatants stood a better chance of walking away from a match still breathing than we generally imagine; Sidebottom estimates they had roughly a 90% chance of surviving a bout, though obviously many exited with injuries. Still, it took a toll on many of the men, and on the eve of battle, the games’ organizers often placed them under a strict suicide watch. But as the historian Seneca notes in one account, such restrictions still couldn’t prevent one particularly depressed gladiator from taking his life by visiting the communal commode and swallowing the sponge kept there for washing up.
—A.B.
Watching:
“
It Was Just an Accident
”
The basic premise of “It Was Just an Accident” makes this Iranian film sound like a slog: After many years, a former prisoner, Vahid (Vahid Mobasseri), seemingly has the chance to exact revenge on Eghbal (Ebrahim Azizi), the man who tortured him for so long. It is more complicated, though, and funnier, too, because Vahid isn’t absolutely positive that the man he’s just confined in a makeshift jail cell—the back of a van—is his former tormentor. Mostly, he’s basing his judgment on the fact that the torturer had a fake leg, and so does the guy rattling around like a box of leftovers in the rear. He decides he needs help verifying the man’s identity and motors around Tehran, enlisting other people he knows who suffered the same fate he did; he even convinces a bride to abandon her wedding. They’re after a dark truth, but with a sharp script and bright-eyed editing, their quest isn’t an unpleasant one to follow from the passenger seat.
—A.B.

标题: The ‘Tokenmaxxing’ Tide May Already Be Turning
内容:
At tech giants like Meta Platforms, employees may have embraced the idea of “tokenmaxxing” as a way to show off how dedicated they are to using AI in their jobs. Don’t expect businesses beyond the deepest-pocketed tech giants to follow their lead, though.
As The Information
reported on Monday
, employees inside Meta have been competing to see who could rank highest on an internal leaderboard called Claudeonomics as the most prolific user of tokens—a basic unit of AI consumption companies like Anthropic use to charge for access to their models. The employee who ranked highest on Claudeonomics used 328.5 billion tokens over a recent 30-day period. That could have translated to a bill of nearly $2 million based on public pricing data.
Even among tech leaders who are brimming with excitement about what AI can do for their businesses, the idea of either encouraging or allowing employees to burn through as many AI tokens as possible was a head-scratcher. “In the rest of the world, yeah, we have to plan ahead and have some kind of rationing of the compute budget,” said Aaron Levie, CEO of Box, which provides cloud-based file-sharing and content-management services.
Businesses everywhere are in the middle of figuring out the best ways to incentivize their engineers and other workers to boost their productivity using AI. It’s hard to find many willing to tolerate the risks of tokenmaxxing to do so, though. Skeptics believe the practice could detonate companies’ IT budgets and encourage employees to game the system so they can inflate their scores.
Even within Meta, some staffers expressed their disapproval of the Claudeonomics leaderboard after The Information’s story came out. “I invite people to do a back-of-the-envelope calculation of the energy consumption this represents,” one Meta staffer wrote in an internal discussion forum, according to a copy of the post seen by The Information. “It would be comical if it wasn’t so tragic.”
By Wednesday, the Claudeonomics leaderboard had vanished within Meta. A company spokesperson
later said
the employee who created the leaderboard took it down, not Meta. Shortly before its removal, the leaderboard showed that total token usage across Meta had risen to 73.7 trillion over the prior 30 days from 60.2 trillion a week earlier.
A Meta spokesperson said the company’s employees are evaluated on the impact they deliver and it doesn’t advocate using individual token data as a primary way to evaluate performance. At the same time, some Meta employees believe the company has sent mixed messages on the topic.
For example, it includes token usage among the data points it uses to track the performance of employees through an internal AI-powered system called Checkpoint. And despite the removal of the Claudeonomics leaderboard, Meta still allows employees to view their personal and team token usage through an official dashboard. A Meta spokesperson said it is using the official company dashboard, AI Insights, to understand how organizations are shifting to AI-native ways of working, adding that it provides insights beyond token usage, including code-related metrics and various other insights.
Skeptics of tokenmaxxing have come up with other ways to get their employees to do more with AI.
Axon, for example—a maker of bodycams, Tasers and other products for law enforcement—offers cash bonuses to encourages its teams to use AI to boost their productivity. Teams inside the company become eligible for the bonuses when they exceed the goals on their individual road maps for the year by a minimum margin of 15%. For the company’s 2,000 or so software engineers, that means using AI programming tools to increase the number of goals they attain can add hundreds of thousands of dollars a year to their compensation, said Josh Isner, Axon’s president.
Isner said Axon’s teams are collectively tracking toward exceeding their road map goals by 30% this year, mostly because of their use of AI coding tools. Axon primarily relies on Claude Code and to a lesser extent Cursor. Isner estimates the company’s spending on those tools will be in the “dozens of millions of dollars” this year. He believes evaluating employees based on token consumption wouldn’t be as effective for achieving Axon’s aims.
“There’s more and more risk when you’re just introducing these measurements of, like, ‘Just use this tool as much as you possibly can and we’ll pay you for it,’” said Isner. “How do you know you’re getting the outcome you want or any outcome?”
Similarly, Levie is using the expected productivity gains from AI tools to raise the ambition level of Box’s product road maps. Whether employees hit those revised goals will play an important part in their compensation. He isn’t encouraging tokenmaxxing and doesn’t anticipate the trend catching on broadly among large companies outside Silicon Valley.
At the same time, Levie said he is enjoying the buzz about tokenmaxxing “as a form of discourse in the industry,” saying it reminds him of long-running debates prior to the advent of AI coding tools over evaluating engineers based on the number of lines of code they produce. AI is so boundary pushing, in Levie’s view, that he is sympathetic to the impulse behind tokenmaxxing, even if it leads to some waste.
“Deep in the Valley right now, we’re in the mode where you have to let the engineers use the tools a bit,” Levie said.
Gaming the System
In some instances, employees at big tech companies are coming up with creative ways to show—or exaggerate—how much they’re using AI coding tools.
Late last year at Amazon, for example, a manager within the company’s sprawling e-commerce organization told engineers on his team they could be using AI coding tools more frequently, according to someone who heard the comments. In response, someone on that team wrote code that made each chat with an AI coding tool called Cline appear to consume 10 times the tokens it normally did, the person said. The team using the trick shot up to one of the top AI-using groups in a subset of the Amazon stores division, the person said.
The cheat code didn’t last. Early this year, the team that had employed the trick found it no longer worked, because Amazon had made the system harder to manipulate.
An Amazon spokesperson said it didn’t have enough information to investigate the episode, but added that as a company, it doesn’t “set nor encourage these kinds of goals.”
“We don’t mandate teams use AI,” the spokesperson said. “However, we expect employees to use resources—including AI—to make work more engaging and improve customers’ lives.”
At Meta, some engineers have undertaken similar efforts to pad their AI usage metrics. Some have done so by instructing their AI agents to generate lots of tiny code changes that don’t meaningfully improve functionality, according to one Meta employee. Each change still counts as a code commit—a saved update to the code base—which drives up token usage.
Other Meta employees have sought to add to their AI usage stats by employing transcription bots to record and take notes during meetings, according to a second Meta employee. In some cases, multiple employees have developed their own transcription tools and encouraged others to use them, since doing so will inflate the token usage of whoever created the tools, the person added.
A third employee said some Meta staffers with low token usage sometimes expressed concern about not being seen as “AI native.” At the same time, in this employee’s view, having very high token usage should not be a status symbol, as it’s not a good measure of productivity.

标题: Netflix on Deck This Week; Pressure on Software Stocks Intensifies
内容:
We interrupt our regular AI programming to talk about something more entertaining: Netflix! The video-streaming giant kicks off tech earnings season on Thursday with its first-quarter earnings. It’s the first quarterly report since Netflix walked away from its $82.7 billion deal to buy Warner Bros. Discovery’s streaming and studio operations, so that distraction is gone.
Now we’re back to guessing how long Netflix can keep growing at its recent annual pace of around 15%,
well above rival streaming firms
like Disney (8% last fiscal year) and WBD (5% in 2025). Netflix forecast 15.3% top-line growth for the first quarter but 12% to 14% growth for the full year. That full year projection is still decent growth, but a more serious slowdown is inevitable, perhaps within a year or two.
Netflix is relying on its small ad business to drive growth right now but that can only last so long. Netflix forecast ad revenue would double to $3 billion this year, out of total revenue it projects will be as high as $51.7 billion. How much bigger can its ad business get? The streaming ad market is not huge—$37 billion in the U.S. this year, estimates eMarketer, compared with $133 billion for U.S. social media. And the market is
extremely
crowded, with everyone from YouTube to Amazon to Disney to WBD jockeying for market share.
Otherwise, Netflix is propping up growth with price increases—
it announced the most recent one in March
—but that comes at a cost. The price of Netflix’s ad free plans have roughly doubled
since 2017
. Raising prices is a good way to drive away subscribers over time, as the cable industry learned. And it’s much easier to cancel Netflix than it is to cancel cable. Netflix no doubt thinks anyone unhappy about the price will downgrade to the tier with ads, which is still cheap at $8.99 a month. But for many people, the ad-free nature of Netflix has always been a big part of the service’s appeal.
Netflix is still the streaming company to beat, but its above-average growth won’t last—nor will its premium stock valuation.
Saas-pocalypse Revisited
How bad can things get in the stock market’s software sector? Mass selling of enterprise software stocks resumed last week after a pause of a few weeks, as jitters about the impact of new AI tools on older software businesses intensified. UBS Securities on Thursday published a report saying that since December, it had been hearing from big companies that growth in spending on AI this year would likely squeeze their spending on other software.
UBS said over half of the calls it has with business customers include anecdotes of “containing” spending on non-AI software. It’s no wonder investors reacted badly. On Friday, ServiceNow and Snowflake were each down around 8% respectively, Salesforce fell 3.5%, and even cybersecurity stocks such as Palo Alto Networks and CrowdStrike were off 6.7% and 4% respectively. (See
our story today
about software companies’ spending on research and development).
Cybersecurity firms until now had been insulated from the investor freak-out, presumably because investors know AI will make it easier for hackers to break into systems, making cybersecurity even more important. Increasingly, however, it seems that AI firms could simply take over cybersecurity themselves.
The most battered stocks, all up, include Figma, a software design firm, which has lost half its value this year and on Friday had an enterprise value of just $7.9 billion—less than half the $20 billion Adobe planned to pay for it in 2022, before antitrust opposition squelched the deal. And Asana, Dustin Moskovitz’s collaboration software tool, is down 60% so far this year.
In Other News
•
Meta Platforms is hiring three senior OpenAI executives who helped lead the ChatGPT-maker’s ambitious Stargate cloud and data center initiative, according to two people with direct knowledge, marking a notable escalation in the
battle for AI infrastructure talent
.
• Tesla won approval to sell its driver assistance software in the Netherlands, the country’s vehicle regulator said on Friday, in a key step toward potentially selling the service in the entire European Union.
• CoreWeave, a provider of cloud computing for AI, on Friday said it had struck a multi-year deal to develop and run Anthropic’s Claude AI models. The company expects the first servers to come online for Anthropic later this year. The two companies didn’t disclose the size of the deal.
Friday on The Information’s TITV
Check out
today’s episode of TITV
for our OpenAI bull versus bear debate.

标题: Atlassian and Figma Lead the R&D Arms Race
内容:
Wall Street’s sell-off of enterprise software stocks resumed in earnest late last week, sending stocks such as ServiceNow and Snowflake down around 8% or so on Friday, as jitters over the impact of new AI tools on older software firms intensified. But the panic may be too indiscriminate.
One way of sorting out which software firms may have more of a future is to look at their spending on research and development as a percentage of revenue, a sign of how deeply they’re investing in new products. On that metric, two firms in particular stand out—Atlassian and Figma.
Atlassian, an Australian software company best known for its Jira application, used for project planning, spent $2.7 billion on R&D in its most recent fiscal year, which ended on June 30, 2025. That equated to 51% of its revenue for the period. Figma, meanwhile, reported R&D costs of over $1 billion in 2025, or 97% of its revenue for the year.
In comparison, the average of nine other companies—including Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot, Palantir, Microsoft, Shopify and Adobe—was about 19% in their most recent fiscal years (see graph). Those other firms allocated more of their spending—on average, 27% of revenue—on sales and marketing.
According to DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria, the focus on sales and marketing by so many software as a service firms is because the value they provide “has a lot more to do with the service than it does with the software.”
But Robert Wilson, an investment manager at asset manager Baillie Gifford, thinks software firms should be investing as aggressively in R&D as Atlassian and Figma.
“I’m losing that fight with the market,” Wilson said. “The market is just in complete risk-off mode in general.”
Wilson said overall, companies’ R&D spending is a good measure of their efforts to build AI apps, agents and features to make their products more competitive. He noted that they may calculate R&D somewhat differently based on how they account for stock-based compensation allotted to engineers.
Software firms face an uncertain future of black-and-white options. AI agents built with tools like Anthropic’s revered Claude Code and OpenAI’s Codex could either render traditional software companies’ applications obsolete or help the firms grow massively.
Most established software firms are introducing their own AI services. Salesforce, for instance, has introduced Agentforce to help customers build and manage their AI agents.
But as the differences in R&D spending demonstrate, not every company is investing heavily in new products relative to their revenue. Atlassian and Figma have said in recent filings that their heavy R&D spending is driven in large part by the costs of developing AI tools and features. Software companies’ R&D costs typically come mainly in the form of compensation to engineers but also include payments for software tools and large language models that engineers use to build new products and apps.
Both Atlassian and Figma may be spending more heavily because they’re worried about their vulnerability to AI. Indeed, Figma is under scrutiny from investors because of concerns that AI tools could easily replace its design software, while Atlassian’s seat-based pricing model could face pressure because AI coding tools like Claude Code may reduce how many users need to use its apps.
Both stocks have fallen more than the average software firm. Shares of Atlassian and Figma are down roughly 65% and 51%, respectively. The benchmark for software stocks in the S&P 500 has fallen 26% so far this year, thanks to worries about the impact of AI on the sector.
To be sure, larger software firms such as Salesforce and ServiceNow are increasing their R&D spending—at least in absolute dollar terms—just not as strikingly as their smaller peers. Salesforce has spent an average of 15% of its revenue on R&D over the past eight years, translating to an increase in R&D spending from nearly $2 billion in 2018 to $6 billion in its fiscal year ending Jan. 31.
Salesforce didn’t respond to a request for comment but said in its securities filings that it expects R&D to be stable as a percentage of revenues as it invests in creating new tech such as AI agents, while its spending on sales and marketing will decline thanks to AI-related efficiencies.
ServiceNow said it “absolutely prioritizes investment in R&D” and it was “maintaining and in many places growing our investment in AI, while also taking advantage of internal AI efficiencies.” ServiceNow’s spending on R&D has increased from around $500 million to roughly $3 billion between 2018 and 2025.
DA Davidson’s Luria argued that it makes sense that the companies’R&D costs as a share of revenue have stabilized in recent years. He believes those expenses aren’t a metric for how well a company is executing its AI strategy.
“Throwing money at the problem is not going to solve it,” he said. Instead, software firms should use AI tools to get R&D projects done more efficiently and bring down costs.

标题: What SpaceX’s Numbers Do and Don’t Tell Us
内容:
The mysteries of SpaceX are no more. My colleague Cory Weinberg today wrote
the second
of
two stories
in four days that pulled back the curtain on the finances of Elon Musk’s space company, which is planning to go public in a couple of months. You don’t have to wait for the formal IPO filing to get the dirt on SpaceX.
The bottom line is that SpaceX as a whole is a money pit, although one of its businesses is not. The Starlink satellite-based internet access business is doing well—it generated $7.2 billion in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization last year. But neither the rocket launch business nor SpaceX’s xAI is producing cash. The AI business is the biggest money-loser, but that’s not surprising given how costly AI development is.
None of this needs to matter to people pondering the SpaceX IPO. After all, this isn’t a normal offering. Elon Musk is asking people to finance his ambitions to compete in AI, to build orbital data centers and to travel into outer space. All are worthy goals for humanity, although they’re not necessarily the building blocks for a profitable company.
As long as investors are clear-eyed about what they’re buying into and the very real chance they will end up losing their money, who can complain? As for
the $2 trillion valuation
that some bankers want to put on SpaceX, it’s beyond analysis. As far as SpaceX is concerned, the sky is the limit. For investors, it depends on what you’re looking for for.
Google is Still the Biggest Ad Platform
Who is the biggest ad company in the world? A projection by eMarketer,
first reported by The Wall Street Journal
on Monday, suggests Meta Platforms will surpass Google this year. That would be a first and a striking size of CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s success. But is it right? Not exactly.
The analysis is based on net revenue—the amount of ad dollars that Google keeps after it  deducts payments to outside websites and YouTube creators. That’s not the same as assessing which company runs the biggest ad platform in terms of marketer spending. On that basis, Google unquestionably is the biggest, as it draws the most ad dollars. In 2025, Google’s global ad revenue was $294.7 billion, while Meta’s was $196 billion.
Meta is growing twice as fast as Google, though, so Meta is likely to eventually pass Google even on a gross basis, although it will be a while. Assuming Meta’s ad business grows 22% in 2026, in line with its growth rate in 2025, its ad revenue would end up at $239 billion this year. Assuming Google’s business expands 11%, in line with last year, its gross revenue would be $327 billion.
To be sure, Meta and Google are converging on a net basis. Google forks over about 20% of its ad revenue to other websites and companies that help expand its reach in one way or another. In addition, YouTube shares a big chunk of its revenue with creators who post videos on the site. Emarketer estimates that Google’s net ad revenue, after deducting both of these expenses, was $214 billion last year and will be just under $240 billion this year. Emarketer estimates that Meta’s ad revenue will reach $243 billion this year.
The net figure matters to Google shareholders and anyone concerned about Google’s margins, to be sure. But each companies’ growth rate may be more important for understanding the future of both companies, and the ad market. And forecasting their growth is difficult.
Both companies are incorporating AI into their ad operations, a move which could help accelerate both companies’ growth rate. Google, though, is dealing with changes to its search business. Both have to contend with competition from OpenAI in ads. Meta may well pass Google but don’t bet on it.
In Other News
•
An OpenAI executive said in a memo to staff Sunday that the company is seeing “staggering” demand for its upcoming joint product with Amazon Web Services and that its exclusive cloud deal with Microsoft had “limited our ability to meet enterprises where they are,”
CNBC reported Monday.
• Tether, the world’s biggest stablecoin issuer, has hired Jeremy Pollack, most recently vice president of Kinexys by J.P. Morgan, the bank’s blockchain business unit, as its institutional expansion manager.

标题: New SpaceX Numbers Show Reliance on Starlink
内容:
A new set of numbers for Elon Musk’s SpaceX makes it clear that the success of its potential initial public offering will depend on investors embracing the growth of the company’s Starlink satellite internet business.
The other two businesses inside SpaceX—rocket launches and AI—are burning cash and not generating the kind of growth that makes investors willing to pay the staggering valuation Musk is seeking, according to previously unreported financial figures viewed by The Information.
SpaceX’s Starlink internet connectivity business generated $11.4 billion in revenue, growing 50% from the prior year and generating 61% of the company’s overall sales, according to the financial figures. The business line was the only one generating cash, the figures indicate.
Starlink’s earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization as well as stock-based compensation were $7.2 billion last year, meaning the adjusted Ebitda margin on those sales was 63%, rising from 41% in 2023 and 50% in 2024.
SpaceX’s space and AI businesses have shrinking margins and are burning cash as the company invests in its next rocket and in data centers.
Last valued at $1.25 trillion, SpaceX has been planning to go public in June in what would likely be the largest IPO of all time. In February, SpaceX bought xAI in a deal that valued the Musk-owned AI developer at $250 billion. The Information
reported last week
that overall the company had a nearly $5 billion net icome loss.
SpaceX is boosting Starlink by launching bigger constellations of satellites into space, allowing it to reach more kinds of customers for internet service, including people in cities who choose it over traditional broadband providers. “They’re blowing away the amount of capacity that people previously had access to,” said Chris Quilty, a satellite and space analyst at Quilty Space, referring to the amount of data that can flow through the satellite network.
Investors will have to consider whether the promise of Starlink outweighs the uncertainties in the rest of the business. The company expects to try to connect the three businesses, arguing that  its dominance of the rocket launch industry will propel its AI business by launching data centers into space.
Last year, revenue in the rocket launch business only grew 8%, to $4.1 billion. Most of SpaceX’s 160-plus launches sent up its own Starlink satellites. Other customers include government agencies and other companies launching satellites for national security or imagery, but that’s been a limited market, Quilty said.
SpaceX’s AI business, which includes the X social media site and model developer xAI, grew revenue just 23% last year, to $3.2 billion a year, putting them far behind Anthropic and OpenAI in growth and size.
The cash Starlink’s business threw off wasn’t nearly enough to support the other businesses. The figures imply an approximation of Starlink’s free cash flow—its adjusted Ebitda minus capital expenditures—at about $3 billion. By that measure, its rocket launch business had negative free cash flow of about $3 billion, while its AI business burned nearly $14 billion.
Overall, SpaceX’s cash burn by that measure would be about $14 billion. Last year, SpaceX put out more on capital expenditures—$20.7 billion—than it generated in revenue.
OpenAI and Anthropic burned significantly less cash than SpaceX last year—$9 billion and $4 billion, respectively, The Information previously reported, although there could be differences in how those companies accounted for cash burn.
SpaceX’s forecasts for this year’s revenue aren’t known.
Its steep valuation, most recently $1.25 trillion, or 266 times last year’s Ebitda, will require serious optimism among investors. That valuation is a significantly higher multiple than those of Meta Platforms, Alphabet and Nvidia, trade at 16, 25 to 36 times last year’s Ebitda, respectively. The closest comparison might be Musk’s other big company, Tesla, which trades at 119 times last year’s Ebitda.

标题: Handshake, Mercor Revenue Surges on Demand for Human Contractors to Train AI
内容:
AI firms’ voracious appetite for more data has lifted sales for obscure startups doing the industry’s unglamorous work: contracting
with lawyers, PhD holders and medical doctors
who grade the answers AI models generate.
The latest is Handshake, a 12-year-old startup that began as a job site for college graduates. Handshake’s gross annualized revenue from AI training has risen to nearly $1 billion, up from $550 million in January and $5 million to $10 million a year ago, a few months after it started the new business, according to two people familiar with its finances.
Its revenue from AI training has risen to nearly $300 million after paying the contractors engaged to do this work. In addition, Handshake is generating close to $150 million in gross annualized revenue from its college recruiting business, an older software business that sells data on job seekers to employers, the people added.
Handshake would become at least the fourth data-labeling startup to generate at least $1 billion in annualized gross sales in recent years. Industry leader Surge AI passed that milestone in 2024,
The Information
reported, and Scale AI reached that level before Meta Platforms acquired nearly half the firm last year.
And Mercor, a three-year-old startup that pays thousands of contractors to train AI models, hit a gross annualized revenue pace of more than $1 billion earlier this year, up from $500 million in September,
The Information
reported last week. Like Handshake, Mercor pays 60% to 70% of those sales to contractors, so its revenue after those contracts has reached $300 million to $400 million. The startup is profitable on a free cash flow basis, according to a person familiar with its finances.
These companies supply AI labs such as Anthropic and OpenAI with customized data generated by human experts employed on a contract basis to stress-test models and answer questions about niche subjects like doctorate-level physics.
Data Breach
Mercor’s growth occurred before a late March data breach, however, and it’s not clear if the company has been able to sustain its revenue pace this month. Meta indefinitely paused its work with Mercor and is internally investigating the security breach, a Meta spokesperson said.
Mercor has said it was “one of thousands of companies impacted” by an attack on open-source application programming interface service LiteLLM. The hack breached companies that use or installed the LiteLLM tool, including Mercor. Following reports of that breach, another hacking group, Lapsus$, has claimed it hacked Mercor and auctioned off multiple terabytes of source code, videos and other data belonging to the company.
Several other startups, including Invisible Technologies and Turing, are also competing to win contracts. Turing, which focuses largely on using contractors to
evaluate models on coding tasks
and other science-based reasoning, notched
$300 million in annualized revenue by the end of 2024
. Another data-labeling startup, AfterQuery, said last week it had surpassed $100 million in gross annualized revenue and was recently valued at $300 million.
While the race to develop smarter AI labs has fueled fast growth, revenue gains aren’t always consistent because it’s fairly easy for the biggest labs to switch data-labeling services. For example, last year
OpenAI said it would stop working with Scale
after the Meta investment. Scale’s recent revenue couldn’t be learned. It now also works on AI applications for enterprise customers.
Some buyers of the training data have started to bring data labeling in-house.
XAI, now part of SpaceX, employs over a thousand remote AI tutors
to train its models.

标题: The Electric: In the U.S. and China, Exaggerated Claims of Solid State Batteries
内容:
In a livestreamed event
last month, China’s Chery Automobile said it had made a breakthrough in solid-state batteries and would deploy them next year in a luxury electric SUV that would have a whopping 560 miles of range.
Chery isn’t the only carmaker claiming to be close to producing a solid-state battery. By my own count, at least 11 Chinese and Western carmakers say they are producing prototype solid-state batteries, are about to test them in an electric vehicle or—in one case—are already using them in a commercial EV. When you also count pure battery makers, the number is much higher.
The claims are important because industry veterans regard solid state as the greatest potential advance for EV batteries since the commercialization of lithium-ion batteries three decades ago. If carmakers could deploy such batteries, they say, the resulting EVs could go 500 miles and more on a charge, and no driver would ever suffer from range anxiety or a battery fire.
But battery experts are skeptical despite the claims and say that while the industry is closer to a working, reasonably priced, manufacturable solid-state battery, no one is truly there yet.
This leaves the industry with the same nickel- and iron-based lithium-ion batteries that have powered EVs since the launch of the industry around two decades ago. Those
“good enough” batteries
have steadily improved, making many industry veterans wonder whether the world still needs solid state.
China’s SAIC Motor, which owns the old British MG brand, has made among the boldest claims for the technology. In December, MG launched the Anxin in China with what it called the world’s first commercial semisolid battery, a variant of solid state, at a price tag of $14,500. MG said it would release the model in Europe this year.
Although described as solid state—meaning a solid separator replaces the usual liquid—the battery has just 5% less liquid than a conventional lithium-ion one. I asked Varnika Agarwal, a solid-state analyst at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, an industry research firm, whether that really counts as semisolid.
“In technical terms, there’s no real definition of what is classified as a solid-state battery,” she told me. “The moment you put in a solid separator, you can call it a solid-state battery.”
She added that so much money has been spent to overcome the technical challenges of solid-state batteries that “companies are now under pressure to deliver it.” In other words, read solid-state announcements with some caution.
The exaggerations around solid state reflect the pressures resulting from the global downturn in EV sales. Even in China, where half of all new car sales are either EVs or plug-in hybrids, sales of electrified models were down 21% in the first quarter year on year. That has led carmakers to try even harder to differentiate themselves from the competition.
For decades, battery researchers have tried but failed to get rid of the liquid electrolyte used in conventional batteries to shuttle lithium ions between the two electrodes, the movement that creates electricity and makes an EV go. The electrolyte is one of the main reasons for battery fires, since lithium can burst into flames when it comes into contact with anything liquid.
Instead, researchers wanted to slip a sheet of plastic or another material—a solid—between the electrodes. If they could do so, they would be able to load far more lithium into the battery without triggering a short or igniting a fire.
In the West, Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz and Toyota are among the carmakers trumpeting plans to deploy EVs powered by solid-state technology in the coming years. In China, BYD and Geely, in addition to Chery and MG, are among the major carmakers promising such cars.
Chery said it had 1,200 researchers working on its solid-state battery, which would power the premium luxury Exeed EX7 SUV. It plans to begin mass production of that model next year.
But a key obstacle to all solid-state batteries loaded with added lithium is dendrites, a mysterious spikey growth that cause shorts and fires; no one has been able to figure out decisively what causes dendrites, nor devise a way to prevent them. Other barriers include high cost and the ability to manufacture solid separators at the speed and volume needed for EV production.
There is reason to believe that there will be no commercial-scale EV powered by a solid-state battery this decade and possibly well into the 2030s. Massachusetts startup SES AI discovered this truth over the last two years. In 2021, the company signed contracts for developing semisolid-state technology with General Motors, Honda and Hyundai, but GM quietly dropped the effort two years ago, and the other two did so last year. SES CEO Qichao Hu told me he knows of no major carmaker that is actually going ahead with large-scale commercialization of such an EV.
VW has touted one of the most advanced solid-state efforts—its partnership with California-based QuantumScape. But QuantumScape’s semi-solid battery delivers an energy density of just 301 watt-hours per kilogram, not much higher than that of state-of-the-art conventional lithium-ion batteries costing much less. And neither QuantumScape nor VW’s battery arm, PowerCo, has shown it can manufacture the battery at large scale.
Madhuri Thakur, senior director of research at California-based battery maker Farasis Energy, has gone to industry conferences and challenged companies making impressive claims about their solid-state batteries. “I was asking, ‘Why are you not producing it? Everything looks so awesome. What is stopping you?’” she said.
She continued, “Normal lithium-ion battery production is so complex, you can just think of how hard it is when you’re going to solid state. It’s very, very complex.”
Last week, BYD’s chief scientist, Lian Yubo,
told an industry conference
that while his company and others in the industry had made important advances in solid state, the technology was still not ready for mass use in EVs. He seemed to be steering the conversation away from solid state.
“We must remain clear-headed,” Lian said. “Solid-state batteries are not the only path for the evolution of power battery technology.”
----
Noteworthy
In the car industry’s latest
retreat, Volkswagen said
it would halt production
of the electric ID.4 crossover SUV in the U.S. The German company plans instead to produce its combustion Atlas SUV at the same Tennessee assembly plant. The ID.4 was the only EV that VW made in the U.S. I took the model on
two test runs
when it first came out in 2021. It was bland, to say the least, and not a product anyone could realistically hope would go viral. And it didn’t.
CATL, the world’s largest
battery maker, is attempting to expand beyond the EV industry, producing batteries to
power big cargo ships
. Since such batteries would be humongous, heavy and expensive, CATL’s idea is to retain ownership of them—vessels with depleted batteries would move into port, where CATL would swap them for charged ones. The ships would be hybrid electric, partly powered by combustion engines.
Battery recycler Ascend Elements
has filed for bankruptcy
. The Massachusetts startup had raised $1.1 billion in equity and grants since its founding 11 years ago, but it foundered on the same high capital costs
that have dogged
other U.S. battery recycling startups.
----
Upcoming events
Monday, April 27—Financing the AI Revolution
Join The Information at
the New York Stock Exchange to hear top executives and investors discuss how the rapid build-out of AI is reshaping tech, finance and capital markets (
RSVP here
).

标题: Microsoft Slowed AI Spending. Now It’s Playing Catchup
内容:
Microsoft is playing catch-up in the AI power race by grabbing big sites in Texas and West Virginia for data centers fired by natural gas. It wasn’t long ago that the company had a big lead.
In the early days of the AI boom, Microsoft’s in-house energy team amassed a 9-gigawatt war chest of grid-powered data center sites that was the envy of the industry, according to people with knowledge of its pipeline and Wall Street tallies of announced projects. That war chest was the equivalent of nine nuclear reactors. Microsoft also held an advantageous place in line for connecting to several regional grids, the people said.
Then in late 2024 and early 2025, as Microsoft’s infrastructure capital expenditures looked like they could exceed the $80 billion it had budgeted that fiscal year, Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood put a curb on spending. Microsoft’s energy team had to walk away from or slow down several data center deals in the works in both the U.S. and Europe.
To an extent not previously reported, Google, Oracle and others swooped in, shortening their waits for power in the Midwest and mid-Atlantic. In some cases AI competitors grabbed actual sites Microsoft had released, and in others they were able to secure grid power because it relinquished its spot in utility transmission queues, according to members of infrastructure teams that negotiated power in those locations.
Oracle claimed a big chunk of available utility capacity for its data center under construction for Open AI in Port Washington, Wis., said a developer involved in those negotiations. Google moved forward more quickly with two Indiana campuses because Microsoft was in wait-and-see mode, another energy professional with knowledge of the hyperscalers’ moves said.
Microsoft would never get back its place in line, although it has resumed developing several of the projects on revised timelines.
Alistair Speirs, the company’s general manager for its Azure cloud infrastructure, said, “Microsoft’s global infrastructure approach is built on flexibility and optionality, based on the near-term and long-term demand signals we see from customers.”
Microsoft acknowledged last October that its Azure cloud business was short of capacity. “I thought we were going to catch up. We are not,” Hood said on an earnings call. Microsoft will be “capacity constrained” through at least the end of its fiscal year this June, she said.
Hood told investors on an earnings call this January, “We need to make sure we’ve got power and land and facilities available” so it can put chips in them when they’re done “as quickly as we can.”
In recent months, as Microsoft stepped up its model development efforts, teams under cloud computing chief Noelle Walsh have signed deals or reached preliminary agreements with third-party developers to fill the computing power gap. These partners, including newer AI infrastructure players Nscale and Crusoe as well as energy giant Chevron and investor Engine No. 1, promised to bypass the grid by building private, off-grid gas-power generation.
The starts and stops were painful for the Microsoft energy team that reported to Walsh, because securing power on the strained U.S. power grid can take years. Several members have left, including Microsoft’s top energy executive, Bobby Hollis, who announced his departure on March 31. Hollis did not respond to a request for comment.
Microsoft’s decision to forfeit power on the grid may have hurt its aim to host AI workloads. “Microsoft is going to fall behind Google” on AI compute capacity, one person with knowledge of the projects told me. “Google has an amazing team. They continue to push.”
Even so, that person and two other infrastructure managers familiar with the projects  acknowledged that Hood’s restraint at a moment of peak AI mania may still prove to be the right call. That’s because Microsoft is putting more of the risk of delivering these data centers on its partners and may be in a position to grab attractive sites in the future when less-well-capitalized land and power speculators fail. This also reduces its exposure to rapid changes in AI hardware and software. Microsoft also has big campuses coming online now, including in Wisconsin and Georgia.
What has surprised a number of infrastructure professionals is how many of Microsoft’s new sites intend to rely on natural gas and operate independently of the grid, given the company’s longstanding commitments to cleaner energy sources and its deep relationships with power utilities. Microsoft contracted with Crusoe for a 900 MW gas-fired data center complex adjacent to the flagship complex for OpenAI and Oracle that Crusoe is building in Abilene, Texas.
Microsoft is in exclusive talks for a 2.5 GW site in the Permian oilfields marketed by Chevron and Engine No. 1. With both Texas projects, the developers offered gas turbine equipment they’d ordered years ago. In West Virginia, Microsoft has a
preliminary agreement with Nscale
, which recently hired Microsoft’s former head of AI infrastructure for Azure, Nidhi Chappell, to power 1.35 GW of top-of-the-line Vera Rubin chips from Nvidia with fleets of Caterpillar gas turbines. The state passed policies designed to speed building.
Self-generated power is typically more costly because it requires more backup power to match the reliability of the grid. If final estimates are too high, some say, Hood could still balk at projects.
Today, Microsoft is again moving fast to expand in Wisconsin and Indiana but is dealing with a more delicate political environment in those states. To address growing local opposition to data centers, Microsoft asked to rescind certain property tax abatements already granted by the town of La Porte, Ind. A pleased City Council member, Tim Franke, said in March that the yearly property taxes from Microsoft now have the potential to “double or triple” the city budget, and LaPorte Schools Superintendent Sandra Wood called the deal “significantly more lucrative.”
The company is also rapidly building again in Europe, spending $30 billion in the U.K. alone and using Nscale to construct a supercomputer that Microsoft has
dubbed
that country's largest as well as other facilities in Norway and Portugal.
In other news:
But it’s another rival’s turn to hit the brakes: Open AI announced it will pause spending on its building plans for U.K. data centers, ironically
also with NScale
.
Simultaneous to this
, Open AI’s infrastructure chief, Peter Hoeschele, and two others have departed to start a new, unnamed company.

标题: ‘Reinforcement Learning Gym’ Startup, Buoyed by Labs’ Appetite For Training Data, Reaches $750 Million Valuation
内容:
AI developers are getting more creative in how they acquire data to train AI models. For instance, they’re paying startups to develop copies of popular apps, like
Salesforce
or
Excel
, to teach models how to use the apps—otherwise known as
reinforcement learning environments or gyms
.
One beneficiary of this trend is
Fleet
, a two-year-old startup that develops and sells those
RL environments
. The startup is now in talks to raise at least $50 million in new funding at a valuation of around $750 million, including the investment, according to a person with knowledge of the talks. That’s more than a seven-times bump from its seed round valuation, which was less than $100 million, the person said.
Bain Capital Ventures
is in talks to lead the latest round, while existing investors
Sequoia Capital
,
Menlo Ventures
and
SV Angel
plan to participate, the person said. (Spokespeople for Sequoia and Menlo declined to comment, while Bain and SV Angel didn’t respond to a request for comment.)
One driver behind the latest round is the company’s strong revenue growth. Fleet has grown from $1 million in annualized revenue as of late last year to more than $60 million in recent weeks, the person said. (The company calculates annualized revenue by multiplying the last quarter’s revenue by four.)
Skeptics might argue that in this business, revenue growth isn’t a reliable way to assess the future. AI labs have been so desperate for training data in recent months that many data and RL environment startups have seen their sales skyrocket, an example of how a rising tide can lift all boats. Over time, AI labs might try to consolidate their data spending into a few providers, which could leave some of these startups stranded.
One way these startups have tried to create a competitive moat is by specializing in certain kinds of data.
Micro1
, for instance, offers robotics-focused training data, while
Mechanize
has specialized in coding. Fleet first stood out from its competitors by focusing on data that would help to train AI to use browsers and computers and has since expanded to other types of apps, said a person with knowledge of the company.
It’s unclear how long-lasting this specialization advantage will be, since it seems like all of these RL environments are expanding beyond their original focuses.
Fleet is also facing tough competition. Not only does the startup face other RL gym providers, but it also has to take on older training data startups like
Mercor
,
Surge
and
Turing
, who are adding on RL gyms to their traditional data labeling services.
Fleet was founded in 2024 by
Nic Ouporov
, who previously was a founding engineer at
Respell
, a no-code startup aimed at helping non-technical users automate tasks, which was acquired by
Salesforce
in January 2024. Ouporov declined to comment for this article.
Here’s what else is going on…
People on the Move
Meta Platforms
is bringing on
Peter Hoeschele
,
Shamez Hemani
and
Anuj Saharan
: three senior
OpenAI
executives who helped lead the ChatGPT-maker’s ambitious
Stargate
cloud and data center initiative, The Information
reported
.
Overheard
OpenAI
CEO
Sam Altman
addressed an attack
on his San Francisco home in a personal blog post on Friday, noting rising criticism of the AI industry and saying there is a need to “de-escalate the rhetoric.”
Policy Watch
OpenAI
is supporting an Illinois bill,
SB 3444
, that would shield the biggest AI companies from liability for “critical harms” caused by their models as long as they did not intentionally or recklessly cause such an incident, Wired reported.
Here’s what else is going on…
Deals and Debuts
See The Information’s
Generative AI Database
for an exclusive list of private companies and their investors.
Semiconductor startup
SiFive
said it
raised $400 million
in a funding round that values the firm at $3.6 billion after the investment.
ShengShu
, which makes AI video models, raised about $290 million in a Series B funding round led by
Alibaba Cloud
.
Elorian
, an AI research and product lab focused on visual reasoning, raised $55 million in seed funding at a $300 million valuation co-led by
Striker Ventures
,
Menlo Ventures
and
Altimer
.
PeakMetrics
, which helps companies detect disinformation campaigns, raised $6 million in a Series A funding round led by
Moneta Ventures
.
CoreWeave
, a provider of cloud computing for AI, on Friday said it had
struck a multi-year deal
to develop and run
Anthropic’s Claude
AI models. The company expects the first servers to come online for Anthropic later this year.
Tesla
has won approval
to sell its driver assistance software in the Netherlands, the country’s vehicle regulator said on Friday, in a key step toward potentially selling the service in the entire European Union.
Federal Reserve Chair
Jerome Powell
and Treasury Secretary
Scott Bessent
on Tuesday
summoned leaders
of the major U.S. banks, including
Citigroup
,
Bank of America
and
Wells Fargo
, to discuss the cybersecurity risks from
Anthropic’s
new model,
Claude Mythos
, Bloomberg reported.
Anthropic
is exploring potentially
designing its own chips
, according to a report in Reuters, though the effort remains in its early stages.

标题: Microsoft Plots New Copilot Features Inspired by OpenClaw
内容:
As Microsoft faces growing competition for business customers from Anthropic, it is developing new features that would make its AI assistant Copilot function more like OpenClaw, the open-source AI agent that has taken the AI industry by storm in recent months.
