A newly created team under Microsoft corporate vice president Omar Shahine is “exploring the potential of technologies like OpenClaw in an enterprise context,” including a team of always-on agents that would be able to work 24/7 on a users’ behalf within Microsoft 365 applications, Shahine said on Sunday in a statement to The Information.
While OpenClaw is seen as risky among enterprise software buyers because such autonomous AI agents
have gone rogue in the past
, product leaders at Microsoft are optimistic the company can build safer versions of the tools that comply with its existing security guarantees, two other people involved in the effort said. Those efforts are still emerging and product plans could change or be scrapped, one of the people said.
CEO Satya Nadella has made it a top priority in recent weeks to overhaul its assistant, 365 Copilot, which Microsoft sells to businesses as an add-on to Office software. Competing products from Anthropic and OpenAI increasingly overlap with what Microsoft offers in Copilot, making it more important that the software giant introduce new features.
For instance, Anthropic earlier this month announced general availability of a new feature that lets Claude connect directly to users’ Microsoft 365 apps to operate in their PowerPoint and Excel documents. And Anthropic on Friday previewed even more new features that will let Claude automate similar work in Word documents, further encroaching on Copilot’s turf.
That highlights the competitive pressure weighing on Microsoft, which has seen its stock fall 24% so far this year, making it the worst performer among big tech stocks. That’s a sign investors are pessimistic about the software giant’s fate as AI transforms the business world, where Microsoft’s software currently dominates.
Microsoft disclosed in January that it had 15 million paying users of Copilot—just 3% of the total number of Office 365 users—although sales divisions are now optimistic that 365 Copilot is starting to gain traction. Commercial CEO Judson Althoff told staff last week that in the March quarter, Microsoft exceeded “audacious” goals for Copilot sales.
The company has been adding features that resemble Anthropic’s Claude Cowork agent to 365 Copilot. In March, for instance, it unveiled the Anthropic-powered Copilot Cowork (Microsoft
pays Anthropic handsomely
for the use of its models).
Nadella last month reorganized the Copilot engineering teams to consolidate those working on consumer and enterprise versions of the app while elevating several executives to report directly to the CEO.
One of those executives—Charles Lamanna—is now overseeing the effort to build OpenClaw-style AI agent features within 365 Copilot, according to the two people with direct knowledge of the effort.
OpenClaw is built to operate a computer the way a person does, with access to and control over specific applications. Many users of OpenClaw have been buying Mac Minis to run the agent, because that enables it to work around the clock without taking over their primary computer. But Microsoft offers virtual machines in its Azure cloud, and last year it announced a new product, Agent 365, that aims to help companies manage AI agents by granting them identities and login credentials for specific apps.
Boosting 365 Copilot
Lamanna, who oversees Agent 365, sees the OpenClaw craze as a way to boost both 365 Copilot and Agent 365, according to one of the people familiar with his thinking.
He recently assigned a team of roughly a dozen engineers to new roles focused on building 365 Copilot features that resemble OpenClaw, led by Shahine.
Shahine said in the statement that “while our exploration is still early, we’re excited to keep learning and experimenting with more intuitive ways technology can reduce day‑to‑day friction so people can focus on the work that matters most.”
Meanwhile, engineers in other divisions, including those reporting to Perry Clarke, executive vice president of Office 365, have been developing versions of OpenClaw that they’re using to automate internal work, according to a third person with knowledge of the work. Those tasks include serving as a product manager’s assistant that oversees the status of projects, the person said.
More broadly, a key goal is to enable 365 Copilot to run autonomously around the clock and work on complex tasks proactively rather than requiring a prompt every time, according to someone involved in the effort. For instance, such a version of 365 Copilot would be able to keep tabs on users’ Outlook email inboxes and calendars and prepare suggested to-do lists for users at the start of each day, instead of only responding to questions a user asks the AI directly, this person said.
Another goal is to make 365 Copilot better at working within Microsoft apps in the background without needing direct oversight: for instance, reorganizing one tab of an Excel spreadsheet while users work on other tabs.
Product leaders working on 365 Copilot have also discussed building agents that resemble OpenClaw but are specialized for specific roles, like marketing, sales or accounting, which would make it more straightforward to limit the permissions the agent needs and render it safer for use inside an enterprise, one of the people said.
Engineers working on those features are now optimistic that some will be ready in time for a preview at Microsoft’s Build developer conference in June, this person said. But the efforts are still nascent and could change depending on whether Microsoft can prove that the products are secure enough for enterprises to use, one of the people said.
Elbowing Out Other Chatbots to Win Customers
Microsoft and Anthropic are increasingly competing for the same turf. Corporate customers have been carefully evaluating their products’ overlapping features as they decide which one is worth their money.
For instance, Alexandre Giess, a marketing executive at German manufacturer Ameco Group, said he has bought several 365 Copilot seats for his team, but he has more recently expanded the Claude subscriptions because he found Claude was better than 365 Copilot at automating Excel spreadsheet tasks. Still, he plans to test out Microsoft’s new Cowork features and see if they stack up to Anthropic’s.
“When I saw [Microsoft’s Cowork] announcement, I’m like, maybe I don’t need to pay for Claude if Microsoft already has Claude embedded,” Geiss said. “If Microsoft does the same job as Claude, I’d probably switch.”
Althoff told staff last week that some of the recent growth in Copilot came from consumption-based Copilot products, in addition to per-seat sales (while 365 Copilot is available as a $30 per month subscription, companies can pay for some Copilot features based on usage). He directed salespeople to pitch customers on ways of using Copilot that would involve AI agents being used repetitively and driving more consumption, according to someone present for the meeting.
Still, some customers who were unimpressed with Copilot early on say Microsoft is facing an uphill battle to win them back. For instance, insurance firm National Life Group first started testing 365 Copilot when it was still in beta, but it opted to reduce its spending on the product in favor of OpenAI’s ChatGPT, according to Nimesh Mehta, chief information officer and head of strategy.
“My early testers of Copilot were saying, ‘It’s completely useless, it hurts my productivity because it takes twice as long to do everything.’ We gave it a two-page document and asked it for a summary and it gave us a four-page document,” Mehta said. “I know it’s a lot better today, and maybe we’d give it a second chance, but I’m not sure it’s worth the battle.”

标题: What Amazon’s Globalstar Purchase Means for SpaceX
内容:
Every now and then we get a reminder that big tech’s pockets are as deep as
the Mariana Trench
. Amazon’s purchase of satellite firm Globalstar offers one of those reminders. Despite spending $200 billion this year on capital expenditures for its AI expansion and committing $50 billion on an OpenAI investment, Amazon can still find a few loose coins in the couch to buy Globalstar.
Amazon is paying about $4.6 billion in cash and issuing stock currently worth around $6.2 billion for the satellite firm. For that, it gets to expand the offering of its yet-to-be-launched Leo broadband satellite service to include direct-to-cell capabilities. That would help Leo compete with SpaceX’s Starlink, which already offers a limited mobile service through T-Mobile in the U.S. and other carriers overseas.
Amazon has essentially acquired an option on the possibility that the satellite mobile market will become a big thing—an outcome
far from certain
. As our story reported, there’s little evidence so far that enough consumers care to pay extra for satellite capabilities to justify the investments required for direct-to-cell services.
That makes sense. The ability to get a satellite signal when you’re in a national park is valuable if you break your leg while you’re hiking, but otherwise you might never think about it. Still, SpaceX CEO Elon Musk has bet he can change that with expanded mobile services such as video streaming. (Watch satellite industry consultant
Tim Farrar discuss the Amazon-Globalstar deal on The Information’s Tuesday TITV
.)
The big winner out of this deal, though, may be Apple, which currently uses Globalstar for the very limited satellite service offered on iPhones. The swirl of uncertainty around Globalstar’s future presented Apple with a quandary: It didn’t want to
buy Globalstar itself
, but it needed the service to keep operating. While critics say Globalstar’s service is slow and less advanced than Starlink’s, Apple wouldn’t have been able to switch over to a rival satellite service easily. Moreover, it has
put a couple of billion dollars
into Globalstar in recent years to help the company put up satellites used for the iPhone service.
Amazon’s purchase means Globalstar now has a financially solid owner, one Apple has dealt with in other business areas (Apple and Amazon already have reached a new Globalstar deal). As it has done in AI, Apple smartly lets other companies do the heavy lifting of making risky investments.
AST SpaceMobile Hit
Amazon’s purchase of Globalstar was bad news for another satellite firm, AST SpaceMobile, which is also aiming to launch a direct-to-cell service later this year in competition with Starlink Mobile.
Unlike SpaceX and Amazon—both of which will soon have their own mobile spectrum once their deals close next year—AST relies on borrowing spectrum for its service from its mobile carrier partners, which include Verizon, AT&T and Vodafone. That means AST will have a disadvantage in future negotiations with mobile carriers compared to SpaceX and Amazon, which probably explains why its shares fell 11% on Tuesday after the Amazon-Globalstar announcement.
Still, AST shares are still up nearly 300% over the past year thanks to broad investor interest in space and an enthusiastic base of individual shareholders. We’ll see if the hype about the SpaceX IPO continues to keep
pushing share prices of its rivals
into orbit.
—Theo Wayt
In Other News
•
General Catalyst appointed Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora to its board, the investment firm’s CEO, Hemant Taneja, said Tuesday.
• Aparna Ramani, a top Meta Platforms executive who oversaw the infrastructure behind its AI models, is leaving the company.
• Anthropic is preparing its next flagship model, Claude Opus 4.7, along with a new AI-powered tool for designing websites and presentations, according to a person with knowledge of the products.
• Deutsche Börse Group, the Frankfurt Stock Exchange operator,
said
it has made a strategic investment of $200 million in Kraken, representing a 1.5% stake in the crypto exchange.

标题: Billion Dollar Grüns Sale Gives Consumer Investors Hope
内容:
When Unilever last week announced its
$1.2 billion acquisition of Grüns
, a three-year-old brand of gummy vitamins, I had a flashback to a decade ago when Unilever bought Dollar Shave Club for almost the same price. That earlier deal marked the heyday of venture-backed consumer exits, which have been few and far between more recently.
The Grüns sale is the latest sign that consumer investing is experiencing a revival. At a time when software stocks have stagnated, bankers seem enthusiastic about taking consumer and internet companies public. Companies that might list within the year include messaging platform Discord, fitness social media Strava, scooter brand Lime and fitness ring Oura, my sources tell me.
Private investors, for their part, have shown they’re keen on consumer startups. Oura’s competitor Whoop just raised at a $10 billion valuation. We
also recently reported
that online fashion brand Quince raised at a $10 billion valuation, following  strong revenue growth.
“There were a couple years where people were really trying to shy away from consumer. In the last 4 or 5 months it’s really picked up a lot,” said Sophie Bakalar, a partner at Collaborative Fund.
Said Bakalar, these have been“the counterpoint to a lot of the AI hype and chaos that we’ve seen,” with consumer valuations driven more by current growth metrics than future goals. The firm is an investor in healthy soda Olipop whose rival Poppi was bought by PepsiCo last year for $2 billion.
Other sales have also buoyed VC optimism, though several didn’t involve venture capital. Last year, Unilever bought men’s grooming product business Dr. Squatch for $1.5 billion from a private equity firm, Summit Partners. Elf Beauty bought
Hailey Bieber’s beauty brand Rhode
for $1 billion almost a year ago. Rhode never announced VC funding.
Yet not all of the recent sales are positive signals. AllBirds, a one-time Sand Hill Road darling once valued at $4 billion, sold for just $39 million last month. The depressed price seemed to be the nail in the coffin for an era of consumer startups that once grew primarily via Instagram ads.
Said Bakalar, “the D2C e-commerce craze was more of a trend than long-term advantage.” These days, she said, brands need to make sure they’re selling both online and in stores.
That was the case for Grüns. The company, founded by former Summit Partners investor and Dr. Squatch
board observer Chad Janis
, started selling the supplements through its website and then sold in
stores like Target
and Walmart.
It had raised tens of millions of dollars from venture firms including Headline, Plus Capital and Sugar Capital—which are now looking at a quick return on their investment.

标题: Anthropic Changes Pricing to Bill Firms Based on AI Use Amid Compute Crunch
内容:
Businesses whose employees are heavy users of Anthropic’s Claude products are likely to pay significantly more for them after the company changed its pricing model in recent weeks to charge business customers based on the amount of AI they use.
The shift, which hasn’t previously been reported, is the latest indication of how Anthropic is managing the booming popularity of its coding and agent products, which increase the costs of running that AI.
With the pricing change, customers of Claude Enterprise, a two-year-old bundle of products meant for large companies that now includes Claude Code and its work assistant, Claude Cowork, will have to pay for the amount of computing capacity they consume while using the software on top of a monthly flat fee of $20 per user, an Anthropic spokesperson confirmed.
Those customers previously paid up to $200 a month for every licensed user, receiving a set amount of discounted token usage for the subscription, says Fredrik Filipsson, co-founder of Redress Compliance, a firm that helps companies negotiate software licensing agreements.
He estimates that for some heavy users, Anthropic’s Claude Enterprise pricing changes will double or even triple their costs.
Several IT executives whose companies pay for Claude subscriptions said they are aware of the pricing changes, which have taken effect in recent weeks. Those companies are tracking whether the changes will result in significantly higher bills from Anthropic, the executives said. (Some Claude Enterprise customers may not see the changes until it’s time to renew their contracts.)
The pricing changes don’t affect businesses that pay for fewer than 150 users, such as customers of Anthropic’s Team subscription for small companies, which costs up to $100 per user per month, according to the spokesperson.
Anthropic introduced the new pricing policy after a spike in usage this year of its AI coding agent, Claude Code, as well as Claude Cowork, an AI-powered tool that can take over a computer to manage workplace tasks across multiple applications.
The Anthropic spokesperson said it made the price change because under the prior system some customers would hit usage limits that interrupted their work, while others didn’t use all the capacity they’d paid for. This change “better reflects how customers are actually using Claude as workloads shift from seat-bound productivity into agentic use,” said the Anthropic spokesperson.
Claude’s surging adoption has been a boon for Anthropic’s business. Annualized revenue from Claude Code jumped to $2.5 billion in February from $1 billion in December and
accelerated in recent weeks
, and the number of weekly active users of Claude Code doubled between January and February.
That growth propelled Anthropic’s revenue pace to $30 billion as of early April,
nearly triple its rate at year-end
.
Increased use typically adds to the costs of running AI models, known as inference. Agents like Claude Cowork are particularly compute intensive because they can work for long periods of time without interruption and can even perform a task on a schedule—say, every 30 minutes.
Anthropic’s recent compute costs couldn’t be learned, but red-hot growth last year more than
tripled its inference costs
, causing its
gross margin to fall short of its expectations
.
If subscribers are regularly hitting usage limits with inference-hungry agents, “the subscription margins just erode away,” said Sudip Roy, co-founder of AI software developer Adaption Labs and former director of inference for AI model maker Cohere. “You kind of price subscriptions for underutilization,” he said.
With consumption-based pricing, he added, “it’s more favorable for the provider, and it’s obviously less favorable for the users,” provided they are consuming a lot of tokens. Adaption Labs has enterprise subscriptions to Claude Code but hasn’t experienced Anthropic’s recent pricing changes.
As the popularity of Claude Code and Cowork has taken off over the past five months, thanks to upgrades in the models powering those products, Anthropic has taken other measures to curb some use.
Earlier this month, Anthropic announced that customers on Claude Code subscriptions would have to pay extra to use third-party agent tools like OpenClaw, a viral open-source agent, because of how much computing power those tools were using. “Capacity is a resource we manage thoughtfully and we are prioritizing our customers using our products and API,” Boris Cherny, head of Claude Code,
wrote on X
when Anthropic made the change.
OpenAI’s Play
OpenAI, meanwhile, appears to be trying to capitalize on Anthropic’s move away from subscriptions. In early April, OpenAI announced a new $100 per user per month subscription that offers more access to Codex than its $20 per month tier but less than its most expensive $200 per month tier.
OpenAI characterized this as a move “to better support the growing use of Codex.” Codex crossed 3 million weekly users last week, according to
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman
.
The pricing change won’t necessarily drive away Anthropic customers. Filipsson, for instance, said so far the Anthropic customers he works with aren’t leaving, as they appear to be satisfied with the productivity gains they’re getting from its software, he said.
“When your product is genuinely sticky at the user level, you can afford to let consumption drive revenue instead of locking people into seats,” he said via email.
The pricing change is the latest sign of Anthropic’s rapid emergence as a top AI provider to businesses.
Big software firms also have been adjusting pricing models in recent years to account for the high costs of incorporating AI features. Salesforce, for instance, introduced consumption-based pricing when it launched its Agentforce product in late 2024, and ServiceNow sells its software on a per-employee basis and charges for AI features based on how much customers use them.
AI coding startups have faced the same challenges. Replit and Cursor overhauled their pricing models last summer after the high cost of running the coding agents pressured margins.

标题: Crypto Firms Seek Access to Anthropic’s Mythos While Shoring Up Defenses
内容:
Major crypto exchanges and custodians including Coinbase and Binance are racing to prepare for powerful AI models like Anthropic’s Mythos, which the AI firm says could be dangerously good at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities.
Coinbase, the biggest U.S. crypto exchange, has been in “close communication” with Anthropic about Mythos, “a highly restricted model not available to the public,” Coinbase Chief Security Officer Philip Martin said. “Mythos, and future models like it, will enable even deeper testing of software and systems at scale. This will accelerate digital threats as well as digital defense.”
Anthropic has given some companies, including large banks, early access to Mythos for testing, but it has not included major crypto firms. In the meantime, crypto companies say they have been testing their systems using earlier models and simulating potential attacks.
At Binance, the world’s largest crypto exchange, the company is testing its systems both from the perspective of an external attacker without access to the source code and directly at the source-code level using AI models. “To support this work, we use both commercially available LLMs [large-language models], including Anthropic’s, and internally developed models that are tailored to our security needs,” said Jimmy Su, Binance’s chief security officer.
Fireblocks, a major crypto custodian technology provider for banks including BNY, said it has already used Anthropic’s prior model, Claude Opus 4.6, to probe for vulnerabilities, and the results have surfaced previously overlooked issues.
“Even on the basis of the previous model, 4.6, it has huge implications,” said Fireblocks’ CEO and co-founder, Michael Shaulov. “With that model, we are starting to see it’s able to find things that no other security audit companies were able to find previously.” He said his company fixed the vulnerabilities identified by the model.
Anthropic’s description of Mythos’ capabilities has sparked concerns at the White House, big banks and big tech firms. Anthropic has begun previewing Mythos to a
select
group of major companies including Amazon, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google and JPMorgan Chase, but has not yet released it widely.
Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon touted the bank’s access to Anthropic’s Mythos model in an earnings call on Monday, saying, “We’re aware of Mythos and its capabilities. We have the model. We’re working closely with Anthropic and all of our security vendors.”
But crypto companies appear to be excluded from the initial group with access to Mythos, industry participants say. Uniswap, which is one of the largest decentralized crypto platforms and holds $3.3 billion in assets, has been trying to get in touch with Anthropic to test its model. Uniswap Labs CEO Hayden Adams took to X to ask for help: “Anyone have good Anthropic connect,” he
said
in a Friday tweet. Adams didn’t respond to requests for comment.
Some crypto executives say they expect Anthropic to grant access to crypto firms before releasing the model more broadly. “Maybe [crypto] is not priority No. 1, but they will get to priority No. 10 before actually releasing it. If that’s the case, we should be fine,” said Fireblocks’ Shaulov.
Anthropic had no comment when asked if its Mythos model has so far identified any vulnerabilities related to crypto or blockchain, as well as whether or when it plans to grant access to crypto firms.
Threats Could Accelerate
Crypto platforms already have a history of hacks and breaches, including some high-profile examples in recent weeks, though some attacks have relied more on tricking people into giving up access to systems than breaches of software itself.
Drift, a decentralized crypto trading platform, this month lost about $285 million to hackers who pretended to work for a trading firm and spent months building in-person relationships with its team. Drift
said
it believes those individuals, though not North Korean nationals, were intermediaries acting on behalf of North Korea.
In a separate incident, crypto exchange Kraken said on Monday that a criminal group is attempting to extort the company after gaining unauthorized access to its internal systems, affecting about 2,000 client accounts. The attackers have threatened to leak videos of the systems with client data, and Kraken has cut off their access and refused to pay, its chief security officer, Nick Percoco, said in a
tweet
.
Researchers say more capable AI models could compress the timeline for discovering and exploiting technical vulnerabilities. “Crypto companies will be one of the major fields to suffer from these new [AI] advancements for attackers,” said Niv Hoffman, co-founder and chief technology officer of Buzz, a Sequoia-backed cybersecurity startup that recently
tested
how existing AI models can already carry out cyberattacks in minutes.
Crypto companies are high-profile targets for hackers, and many crypto infrastructures are open source, making them particularly vulnerable to attacks, he said. “Now, because of the exploitation capabilities of AI models, attackers will be able to exploit the next vulnerabilities in cryptocurrency in hours or even in minutes,” Hoffman said.
By contrast, longer-term risks to crypto industry security such as quantum computing remain more theoretical. Google researchers recently warned in a paper that quantum computing, which could break the encryption that keeps bitcoin and other crypto secure, may be a more significant threat for bitcoin than previously thought.
For now, the risk advanced AI models pose “is certainly a bigger problem today than quantum,” said Alex Thorn, Galaxy Digital’s head of firmwide research. He
added that
no such computer exists today and there is no imminent threat from quantum computing.

标题: Uber CTO Shows How Claude Code Can Blow Up AI Budgets
内容:
Uber’s surging use of AI coding tools, particularly Anthropic’s Claude Code, has maxed out its full year AI budget just a few months into 2026, according to chief technology officer Praveen Neppalli Naga.
“I'm back to the drawing board because the budget I thought I would need is blown away already,” Neppalli Naga said in an interview.
Neppalli Naga’s comments highlight the challenges for companies trying to take advantage of new AI tools while also keeping costs under control. Uber is one of a number of companies that say they have encouraged employees to use AI as much as possible, creating internal “leader boards” ranking software engineers who use the most AI.
The rideshare giant uses both Claude Code and Cursor. But since the end of last year, Uber’s Claude Code usage has soared, while usage of Cursor has “kind of plateaued,” though still high. Neppalli Naga said Uber is also planning to “try out” and give engineers access to OpenAI’s Codex.
He wouldn’t disclose exact figures of the company’s software budget or what it spends on AI coding tools. Uber’s research and development expenses, which typically reflect companies’ costs of developing new AI products, rose 9% to $3.4 billion in 2025 from the previous year, and the firm said in a recent securities filing it expects that cost will continue rising on an absolute dollar basis.
Uber uses Claude Code to generate code used in Uber’s backend systems that match drivers and riders, determine prices for rides, improve features, and fix bugs, among other things. It’s also using Claude Code and other AI coding tools to create new AI agents and features, such as its recently-launched Earner Assistant, an AI agent that helps drivers navigate to the busiest parts of cities and towns so they can take high-paying trips.
Neppalli Naga said about 11% of real, live updates to the code in its backend systems are being written by AI agents built primarily with Claude Code, up from just a fraction of a percent three months ago.
The executive said he envisions AI agents taking on the full roles of software engineers—AI agents writing code as well as testing and deploying it in Uber’s backend systems. He also said Uber will try to build supervisor AI agents to check whether other AI agents have made mistakes.
“The vision for me as a CTO is to transform from software engineering to [AI] agent software engineering.”
While Neppalli Naga said in a presentation at the HumanX tech conference in San Francisco last week that he’s not thinking about slowing hiring of software engineers at this point, his comments during the interview suggest that could change.
“We continue to calibrate hiring based on broader business needs,” an Uber spokesperson added.

标题: The Enigma of the Data Labeling Startup Explosion
内容:
If you’re like me, you’ve been watching the explosion of data labeling and reinforcement learning environment startups with a mix of confusion and awe.
These companies, which hire experts in fields like medicine, law and software engineering and build fake copies of apps like
Salesforce
and
Excel
to produce training data for AI models, are
rapidly growing both in terms of revenue and valuations
. But their moment in the sun may soon be about to pass.
These startups seem to lack any sort of long-term defensible moat. Their contracts with the big AI labs varies—some labs, such as
Anthropic
, have tried lots of different startups, researchers say. Individual teams or individual researchers often strike their own contracts with data labeling startups. At other firms, such as
OpenAI
, a centralized data team manages relationships with data labeling firms.
These smaller contracts can range anywhere from hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars. That means data startups can fairly quickly get from zero to $10 million in annualized revenue, researchers tell me.
That could soon change. Now that the labs have had some time to test out a variety of data providers, they could start consolidating their contracts with the few providers that they like the most. In fact, as AI labs like OpenAI and Anthropic move towards going public, public market investors might want to see a more disciplined approach to spending on training data.
AI labs might also want to move some of their most advanced data collection efforts in-house, given recent high-profile security breaches, such as the one at
Mercor
, the $10 billion expert network.
Investors would likely welcome this shift. As things are now, investors have a hard time figuring out the sustainability of the revenue these data labeling startups are bringing in, they tell me.
We’ll have to see how all of this plays out for the data labeling startups. You might think that the AI labs would consider acquiring some of these startups. But researchers at the labs aren’t thrilled at the prospect of having to manage hundreds or thousands of human data labelers in the tedious data gathering process, they say.
And having a fragmented industry of data providers works out for the big labs because those providers compete against each other to offer lower prices, researchers say. Perhaps some startups will end up combining forces if the AI labs get more disciplined about their contracts.
Here’s what else is going on…
Overheard
Anthropic’s
“story is built on fear, restriction, and the idea that a small group of elites should control AI,” wrote
OpenAI’s
chief revenue officer,
Denise Dresser
, in a memo sent to staff on Sunday, according to The Verge. “Their strategic misstep to not acquire enough compute is showing up in the product. Customers feel it through throttling, weaker availability, and a less reliable experience.”
Dresser went on to argue that Anthropic’s “coding focus gave them an early wedge. But you do not want to be a single-product company in a platform war.”
Policy Watch
Anthropic
hired the lobbying firm
Ballard Partners
to advocate on its behalf on matters related to military procurement days after the
Pentagon
declared Anthropic a supply chain risk, Bloomberg reported. The prominent lobbying firm has strong ties to the Trump administration.
Deals and Debuts
See The Information’s
Generative AI Database
for an exclusive list of private companies and their investors.
Sygaldry
, which aims to bring quantum hardware to AI data centers, raised $105 million in Series A funding led by
Breakthrough Energy Ventures
and $34 million in seed funding led by
Initialized Capital
.
Bluefish
, an NYC-based AI marketing platform, raised $43 million in Series B funding led by
Threshold Ventures
and
NEA
.
Helical
, a Gare, Luxembourg-based AI lab for biology, raised $10 million in seed funding led by
redalpine
.
Thinking Machines Lab
has hired
Rudolf Laine
and
Luke Drago
, the co-founders
Workshop Labs
, a one-year-old startup that develops software for personalizing AI systems, and has
bought the startup’s technology
.
OpenAI
acquired
Hiro Finance
, an AI-powered personal finance app backed by
Ribbit Capital
,
General Catalyst
and
Restive
. The startup will shut down on April 20 as founder
Ethan Bloch
and the team join OpenAI.
An
OpenAI
executive said in a memo to staff Sunday that the company is
seeing “staggering” demand
for its upcoming joint product with
AWS
and that its exclusive cloud deal with
Microsoft
had “limited our ability to meet enterprises where they are,” CNBC reported.
Meta
is developing an AI version of
Mark Zuckerberg
that employees can interact with, the Financial Times reported.
Stanford’s
center on human-centered AI released its annual report. The report found that experts and the public disagree about the impact AI will have on jobs, the economy and medical care. The U.S. was the country with the lowest trust in its government’s ability to regulate AI.
OpenAI
announced its first permanent London office, which will house over 500 people, according to CNBC.

标题: Polymarket Reverses Course on Startups That Piggyback on Insider Trades
内容:
Polymarket was already under pressure to curb insider trading when it launched a program late last year to support startups that sent trades to its prediction market. Those companies quickly began handing suspected insider trading accounts to their own customers, letting them copy those trades.
Now Polymarket has launched an audit of those startups. Among them are Kreo, which promises to help users “find insiders before the rest,” and Polycool, which offers a “guide to Polymarket insider trading” on its website.
Polymarket recently launched the audit but wouldn’t say when it began. The Information recently raised questions about the startups in the developer program, which has driven an increasing amount of growth to the prediction market.
Kreo and Polycool are two of a growing number of startups that make apps traders use to place bets on Polymarket’s prediction market. Polymarket in November launched a Builders Program allowing outside developers to create new apps built on top of its technology. Developers it reviews and verifies can get up to $2.5 million in grants and weekly financial rewards for building applications such as new trading systems, automated bots or interfaces enabling users to bet within Polymarket.
Almost 200 developers are now part of the program. The company promotes higher-performing developers on social media, gives program members access to its engineering team and allows a higher volume of trades for verified developers.
Many of the highest-volume developers are copy-trading apps, according to Polymarket’s official online dashboard promoting members of its Builders Program. These copy-trading apps give their customers lists of Polymarket traders with good winning streaks, or flag unusually large or oddly timed bets that may be based on confidential information. Customers can use bots to copy the trades or can receive notifications of seemingly well-placed trades. The apps charge a fee to use their services.
“This isn’t the stock market, where using nonpublic information will land you in jail,” Polycool’s insider trading guide says. “The rules for decentralized prediction markets are a completely different game.”
Anonymous people run most of the copy-trading apps, and at least two have been hacked in recent weeks.
PolyCop, the highest-volume copy-trading service on Polymarket, describes itself as a bot “designed to copy trades from smart money addresses” and touts the success of a user who turned $305 into $45,902 in 30 days using its copy-trading strategy. A PolyCop representative, self-identified on Telegram as Chloe, said six anonymous engineers staffed the company. Chloe said, “It’s pretty common for developers to stay low-profile” when building Telegram-based trading bots.
Polymarket and rival prediction market Kalshi exploded in popularity during the 2024 U.S. presidential election. Trading volume has soared since then—on bets covering sports, the weather and U.S. military activity. Volume is up tenfold in the past year to roughly $23 billion in March.
Polymarket is raising funds, including from New York Stock Exchange parent Intercontinental Exchange, reportedly seeking a valuation of $20 billion, up from $9 billion in October. Kalshi secured funding at a $22 billion valuation in March. Both have secured partnerships with major news and sports organizations to promote their prediction markets. But the explosive rise of betting on the platforms has them on a collision course with state lawmakers who have argued the companies are offering illegal gambling services that bypass state-level laws.
At the same time, the platforms are facing pressure to limit bets from people with confidential information. Polymarket last month sealed a partnership with data analysis firm Palantir Technologies to help ensure integrity in sports wagering. It also updated its rules to ban trades based on “stolen confidential information” and those from bettors who “hold a position of authority or influence” over a particular event.
Kalshi said it is launching new tools to preemptively block people such as politicians and athletes from trading in their respective markets, as well as adding a whistleblower feature to identify rule breakers.
Trading Conundrum
Insider trading presents a curious problem for the companies. On the one hand, Polymarket and Kalshi have promoted prediction markets as a more accurate measure of public sentiment and forecasting than mainstream media and traditional polling. They have argued that people putting their money where their mouth is creates a more reliable indicator than traditional polling in cases such as election odds. But several high-profile cases where it appears bettors have used confidential information have sparked concerns the platforms have created avenues for insider trading that are not policed in the way traditional securities markets are.
Polymarket’s audit of its Builders Program is designed to ensure that third-party developers align with the company’s efforts to make sure its platform isn’t promoting insider trading, according to a person close to Polymarket. It has begun to remove Polymarket branding on social media from some of the developers mentioned in this article, the person said, adding that many of their apps did not get awarded a slice of Polymarket’s $2.5 million grant pool.
Suspicious trades are continuing to draw attention to the apparent insider-trading problem on the platform. On April 7, four user accounts made $663,000 betting on a U.S.-Iran ceasefire. All four of the users created and funded their accounts that same day, had no prior wagering activity and made bets with big potential payoffs, according to blockchain analysis firm Lookonchain.
Trading volume driven by developers in Polymarket’s Builders Program, which launched late last year, has jumped from $100 million in November to more than $600 million in March, according to data provided by the company. Last month, that accounted for 16% of Polymarket’s total volume. The founder of PolyGun, one copy-trading service, recently claimed to have driven as much as 11% of Polymarket’s total weekly transaction volume.
Kalshi launched a similar developer program in November. Anonymous trading hasn’t been a major issue for that company because it verifies the identity of its users and tells developers they must identify their customers. Kalshi has taken action against some high-profile insider traders, such as an editor for social media star MrBeast who traded around $4,000 on markets related to the streamer.
Polymarket doesn’t require customers to submit identification before using its services, and many traders access the site by using virtual private networks to mask their locations and switch accounts.
For customers, using the copy-trading apps requires a leap of faith. Many developers don’t list their owners or staff members and can only be contacted via the encrypted messaging platform Telegram, where anonymous admins warn users to be wary of fake bots impersonating companies or support employees. Some trading bots are also hosted entirely on Telegram.
One developer who chose not to be anonymous is Edward Ridgely, co-founder and CEO of Stand, which offers copy-trading services. “Whenever you’re dealing with people’s money, you have to be transparent,” Ridgely said in an interview. “I personally wouldn’t trust a product where I wouldn’t know the people behind it.”
He said about 40% of his users had tried copy trading. “If you’re looking at copy trading, people inherently want to have the ability to have a magic remote where they say, ‘Make these trades,’ and another actor or agent acts on their behalf,” Ridgely said. “That’s only going to get more sophisticated over time.”
Anonymity has not kept traders away from PolyGun, which listed three pseudonymous leaders: CEO “Esquire,” Chief Operating Officer “Ice” and Chief Financial Officer “Larry The Whale,” in a
press release
last month. The platform appears to be mostly run by one individual, Justin Hunter, who has said he founded a multimillion-dollar social media marketing business in high school, according to archived social media profiles.
Hunter operates multiple accounts on a Telegram channel for PolyGun, including an account for “Larry The Whale.” He said personal security was one of the motivations for maintaining anonymity.
PolyGun, which allows users to follow profitable Polymarket wallets and replicate their trades, processed $35 million worth of trades in February, according to Polymarket—the third-highest share of trading volume for developers on the platform that month. In February, about $70,000 disappeared from PolyGun user accounts after a hacker exploited a vulnerability in its code base, according to a Telegram message posted by “Larry The Whale” in the company’s channel. PolyGun promised to investigate the issue, refund users and launch a security audit of its platform. Hackers earlier this year attacked another copy-trading app, Polycule, stealing roughly $230,000 in user funds, according to an alert sent to users.
Despite the risks, developers are drawing in customers with slick online marketing. Polycool’s website points to the anonymous account “ricosuave666,” which in June turned a small wager into $155,000 by betting on specific questions regarding Israeli military strikes on Iran. “If you’re not paying attention to these large informed moves, you’re not just missing out—you’re probably on the wrong side of the trade,” Polycool says.

标题: Figma’s Apple Moment; Allbirds’ AI Pivot
内容:
AI really is transforming the world! The latest example: Dying shoemaker Allbirds is turning itself into an AI chip firm called NewBird AI. Its stock rocketed 600% in response. But before we dig into that bizarre story, we should highlight what may be today’s more meaningful news: Anthropic executive Mike Krieger’s departure from the board of Figma.
This might be what you’d call Figma’s Apple moment. For young folks, we’re talking about the then-Google CEO’s summer-2009 exit from the Apple board. He left a month after Google
introduced the Chrome operating system
and 11 months
after Google’s Android
first showed up powering a mobile phone. Both moves put Google into direct competition with Apple, something then-CEO Steve Jobs
pointed out to explain
why Schmidt was leaving Apple’s board.
Krieger’s exit from the Figma board echoes that episode. He departed on Tuesday, the same day
The Information reported on Anthropic’s plans
to introduce a new tool for designing websites, which would compete with Figma’s offerings. While that timing was likely a coincidence, Krieger surely knows the two companies are headed for a more intense rivalry.
Until now, Figma and Anthropic have seemed more like business partners. Figma has been actively pitching its products as ideal for working with Anthropic’s Claude Code. Take this blog post
from February
, which carried this subtitle: “Now you can take workflows that start in Claude Code even further in Figma.” The problem for Figma is that whatever advantages its software offers, some designers are likely to find that simply using Claude is easier. Anthropic has figured that out and so have Figma shareholders—which explains why its stock has dropped 45% this year. What’s next for Figma?
Allbirds’ Pivot
Possibly the only thing more boneheaded than Allbirds’ pivot into AI was Wednesday’s investor reaction. It makes you think that if a shoemaker can do so well jumping into a highly technical, competitive arena it knows nothing about, why aren’t other apparel makers or even food companies following suit?
It’s not clear whose idea this was, or whether anyone from Allbirds’ management and board will stick around to see it through. Allbirds, of course, announced a couple of weeks ago it was selling its money-losing shoe business to a fashion brand for just $39 million. The company could have just distributed to shareholders whatever money was left after payments of debts and other liabilities and called it a day.
But no. Allbirds’ management knows a hot trend when they see one, even if power-hungry AI chips are a massive departure from the environmentally friendly approach to shoes the company championed. It is raising up to $50 million in a convertible note offering to fund the AI chip diversification. (
Bloomberg’s Matt Levine has a smart column
pointing out that the investor in the convertible offering will do very well if the stock stays at its new elevated levels.)
A bunch of other companies, mostly crypto miners such as CoreWeave, Crusoe and TeraWulf, have already jumped into AI chip rental (the neocloud business), as we have written about
here
and
here
. Still, pivoting from crypto mining to AI isn’t crazy, given that both industries involve running servers doing intense computation (not that it’s
an easy change of direction
). Allbirds, though, is a whole different story. Neither the management nor the board has experience in AI chips, as far as we can tell from their online bios.
Perhaps the board sees this transformation as a way to wring more money out of the public listing. Maybe the idea is to build up a bit of a business and then sell to an existing player. That might work. But, as Sam Lessin wrote in The Information in 2018,
pivoting a business like this has hidden costs
.
In Other News
•
Meta Platforms is reorganizing its hardware division, Reality Labs, in a bid to “adapt and execute faster” (
more here
).
• Snap said it was laying off about 16% of its workforce, or about 1,000 people, in an effort to reduce its cost base by $500 million and “establish a clearer path to net income profitability,” CEO Evan Spiegel
said in a blog post
. Snap also foreshadowed its first-quarter earnings release, revealing that
revenue rose 12%
.
• ByteDance’s cloud computing unit has made the company’s Seedance 2.0 AI video-generation model
available globally
to enterprise customers in more than 100 countries and regions around the world—but the U.S. is not among them.
• Billionaire crypto entrepreneur Justin Sun, a prominent investor in crypto projects launched by President Donald Trump and his family, on Wednesday labelled a proposal by one of those projects–that would lock up trading of a key token owned by investors–a “scam.”

标题: Apple Sends Siri Staffers to Coding ‘Bootcamp’ in Latest Shakeup For Organization
内容:
Apple is sending a portion of its Siri programmers back to coding school just two months before the company is expected to unveil a major, AI-powered revamp of the voice assistant, people familiar with the team said.
The company plans to send a significant chunk of people working on Siri—a group that totals in the hundreds—to a multi-week bootcamp to learn to code using AI, according to the people. The group of programmers that will go to the bootcamp is expected to be less than 200, one of the people said.
The changes will leave around 60 members of the core Siri development team, while an additional 60 will work in a group that evaluates how Siri is performing, including handling commands from users and whether it’s meeting Apple’s safety standards, one of the people said.
The move suggests that
Apple
feels that a portion of the Siri organization needs to tune up its skills to take advantage of fast-moving changes in programming. AI coding assistants such as Anthropic’s Claude Code and OpenAI’s Codex have upended the programming profession, allowing experienced software developers to produce far more code than they have in the past.
In some parts of Apple, such as its software engineering organization, those AI coding tools have taken off, prompting some of its teams to allocate large budgets for Claude Code, other people familiar with Apple’s use of AI tools said.
The company’s Siri group, though, has developed a reputation as a laggard inside Apple. Over its 15 years in existence, the team has become known for its bloat and divisive politics. The group has had difficulty keeping the voice assistant competitive, especially in recent years with the rise of OpenAI’s ChatGPT and other similarly advanced large-language models.
Apple has made repeated efforts over the years to reorganize the Siri to address its underlying problems. Most notably, early last year, the company took the Siri team out of Apple’s AI organization, then headed by John Giannandrea, and placed it in the hands of software chief Craig Federighi. Apple tasked Mike Rockwell, the leader behind the company’s Vision Pro headset, with directly overseeing the Siri product under Federighi.
That reorganization occurred after Apple revealed an embarrassing delay to the launch of a new Siri, which it originally planned to release in early 2025. Apple is expected to finally debut its long-awaited Siri revamp at the company’s annual Worldwide Developers Conference in June.
The new version of Siri will be powered by Google’s AI model, Gemini, and is designed to offer a more conversational version of the voice assistant that will have the ability to directly answer questions, provide emotional support to users and help accomplish tasks such as booking a trip. There is still a lot of work to do, including on Google’s side, which Apple is in discussions with to host its servers to run the new Siri, The Information previously reported.
Separately, Giannandrea, who announced his retirement from Apple in December, will work his last day as an advisor at the company this week, Bloomberg
reported Sunday
.

标题: The Hidden Costs of AI Shopping Payments
内容:
Shoppers using AI are seeking refunds and disputing credit card charges at an elevated rate, highlighting the financial risks of AI-powered shopping, according to Carlo Bruno, vice president of product at payments firm Adyen. Companies must figure out how to handle that risk before AI can reliably tackle online purchases, Bruno said.
The technology hasn’t had a “big bang” moment yet, Bruno said. “Everyone is thinking of agentic commerce as some massive launch moment, that one day we have bots transacting for us,” he said, but the process will be more gradual. The complexities and risks involved could also prompt payment processors to contemplate charging higher merchant fees on AI purchases, he said.
Early AI shopping features like OpenAI’s now-mothballed ChatGPT checkouts have
struggled to wrangle product data
, and most still have human shoppers ultimately pulling the trigger on the purchase. As fully autonomous agents start shopping, things will get even messier. “The demo looks really slick and really cool, but scale is something else,” Bruno said.
AI shopping is already straining older commerce infrastructure, since AI tools operate at a speed and scale existing systems weren’t designed for. Tools like OpenClaw and Claude Cowork that take actions directly in users’ browsers are only intensifying that pressure.  “A lot of the challenges have been around forever. It’s just that right now they are exploding,” Bruno said.
And while companies including OpenAI, Google, Stripe and Shopify are jockeying to establish competing AI-shopping protocols, there are still questions around shopper adoption and merchant readiness for the new technology. Adyen, a publicly traded payments firm that works with large merchants like H&M and Lululemon and did around $1.6 trillion in payments volume in 2025, has a broad overview of how the next steps might play out.
Adyen, for its part, sees an opportunity in managing data that can feed into a range of AI shopping tools and using its own trove of shopper data to flag rogue AI transactions, though credit card firms will need to play a role as well, Bruno said.
The following is a transcript of The Information’s interview with Bruno, edited for clarity and length.
Q: What’s going on in agentic commerce?
A:
There’s something obviously extremely exciting happening—the AI revolution but also the effect it might have on commerce. Not just what everyone right now is envisioning around the human-in-the-loop flow, basically another interface we could shop through, but also machine-to-machine payments, autonomous agents transacting with agents. That is definitely some distant future that’s actually not that distant anymore.
There’s something happening, though—the demo looks really slick and really cool, but scale is something else. We’re seeing that the production engineering behind this is really hard. We as humans, when we shop, when we transact, we’re going in a pretty linear flow. Agents—they go in loops, in lots of queries in a second. Many requests happen: combinations of size, color, availability of shipping methods, you name it. It’s a big complexity that’s taking place. I call it programmatic shopping almost. Compared to human shopping, it’s just very different.
You can have all of these flows working at the surface, but behind the scenes we see fragmented systems, legacy stacks. There are very few retailers and enterprise merchants around the world that have an extremely clean, high-quality centralized data source.…We’re seeing that the stacks we have today are not designed for that load.
Right now everyone is thinking of agentic commerce as some massive launch moment, that one day we have bots transacting for us. Reality is, it will arrive piece by piece, replacing some things that we knew were forever broken or not working, but also introducing very new logic.
Q: Does there need to be one AI shopping protocol that’s the winner?
It’s very good that many different companies from many different angles are approaching it their way. So that competition, that innovation from many different areas, I think that’s a good thing. At some point, you would expect standardization or at least interoperability. That is the way to make this scalable.
With our seat at the table talking to the Googles and the OpenAIs of the world, we’re advising and also representing the merchant. What we firmly believe at Adyen is two things: first of all, merchant autonomy. We want to reduce the risk of the merchant being an API [application programming interface] endpoint. There is a relationship there with you and me as consumers that our merchants find extremely important.
And the other thing is, I call it ensuring architectural reversibility.…The creators of these protocols will be the first to admit that this is all in motion, in progress.
Q: How does Adyen fit into all this?
We have a unique place right now between merchant systems and the AI platforms, where from two angles, I see a real problem that Adyen is trying to solve.
One is, can we help centralize what we call the product feed, the product catalog of our customers, and expose that to all these different agents? So one central place in which the merchant can drop down all the information, pricing, inventory, whatsoever, and we will take care of the translation to the right interface. All these incoming agents from very different platforms with very different protocols, flows, it all comes down to the same—there’s a commerce request, an agent incoming that has a certain query and needs to be exposed to certain information.
And then the transaction comes in, and then there’s obviously the trust and intent piece, where it’s very, very close to what we’re doing today anyways. It’s just that we have to make a shift as an industry that normally you would block bots and now you’re going to welcome bots, which is a very interesting proposition.
Q: Visa and Mastercard are deploying tools to help agents demonstrate user intent or authorization. What role does Adyen play in mitigating AI-related risks?
We’re definitely building around it. We have a unique position in the middle of that flow. So [we] very much orchestrate all the information that’s happening in real time and use our network. You have a history on our platform, you’re giving agents a certain intent and a certain right to use your payment methods somewhere. There’s a lot of context that we have access to.
The [card networks] play a massive role in the whole game of liability. What happens, not just when fraud happens, but also when you book the flight to Amsterdam, but you’ve got to lay over in LA? Who’s liable? What’s happening? We haven’t figured [that] out as an industry, and I think that’s where the card [networks] will play a very important role.
Q: What are the bottlenecks to adoption in general right now?
In terms of merchants, there is real interest. What I think we’re all trying to figure out is true consumer demand and consumer behavior. Is there going to be a big bang? No. There might be certain industries and certain flows and certain interfaces where this will work perfectly and magically. There might also be very obvious flows, human-in-the-loop flows, for example, where we don’t see this take off.
And that is where everyone is still trying to figure out: What are those hooks, what are those paths, and that big bang, that demo we’re all seeing, that won’t be something that’s being realized in the coming months? I think it will go in bits and pieces, and we’ll find our way testing around it.
Agents, if the data is 99% correct, that’s not enough for the agent. That deterministic truth is extremely important. That is something that some tech players will have very quickly available, and you think specific platforms, for example, there are definitely some merchants and industries where they will go faster. But there are also a couple of verticals where I feel there’s much more investment needed.
What I’m seeing on the other side, definitely there are [merchants] that love to experiment. They are like, “OK, let’s go. I want to be there. I want to be first. I want to be part of whatever product you have running.” There is also a group that says, “Give me that hard data first on impact, and then I’ll go from there.”
Q: Will AI-driven shopping result in higher merchant costs on those purchases?
I see two dimensions. One, there’s an additional player in the mix. That’s something that we’ve got to account for—in this case, AI platforms.
And back to trust and intent, we haven’t yet figured out how much this is going to cost us when it comes to potential damage. The potential for this to also create financial damage is also out there. So how it will turn up in pricing, I don’t know, but there are definitely considerations here. In very early tests, charge-back rates are a little bit elevated. Refund rates are elevated. The agent’s expectation based on consumer intent and the actual execution might be different. That is all that we’re figuring out. And that somehow might have to be reflected in pricing.
Can we treat agentic payments as just another payment flow? We have today shopper-initiated payments and merchant-initiated payments, you in a store versus a subscription payment charge. Now you have agentic-initiated payments. The more we can fit that into existing infrastructure, the more we win when it comes to scalability and basically leveraging existing frameworks. But there are definitely gaps.
Q: What about with OpenClaw or Claude Cowork specifically, tools that are in the browser, mimicking a human user?
Then machine-to-machine payments in whatever form, whether that is indeed an agent or Claude that on your behalf starts browsing, this is extremely interesting. True autonomous payments, making payments invisible seems to be something that in many business models is extremely promising.
We [have] got to find a way as an industry to basically build scalable logic that allows us to verify whether or not just it’s a safe and correct transaction being executed by a machine to machine, but also if it truly follows the intent of you as a consumer and an intent, right there in that situation, that doesn’t have the human in the loop.
Our footprint that we have of consumers across the world—there is a lot of intent in there and there’s a lot of patterns in there. Together with the frameworks that we’ll probably need with the [card networks] to capture what is being requested, and there’s an historical context around that particular request, that combination could be an extremely powerful solve to help enable true autonomous, invisible payments.
Q: I’m sure you see the people who are posting “My OpenClaw booked my flights or returned my package.” What do you make of that?
There are some new challenges, but a lot of the challenges have been around forever. It’s just that right now they are exploding—liability, intent, all the trust factors, but also the load on systems.

标题: I Helped Pick Stocks for the Dow. I’m Watching the SpaceX IPO Carefully
内容:
Please join The Information at the New York Stock Exchange on Monday, April 27, for our “Financing the AI Revolution” forum. Hear from top executives and investors on how the rapid build-out of AI is reshaping tech, finance and capital markets. Learn more
here
.
SpaceX’s financial challenges have become clearer in the past week, thanks to new reporting from my colleague Cory Weinberg. Last week, he broke news on SpaceX’s
nearly $5 billion loss
last year, and on Monday, he scooped details on the
growth of Starlink
and the losses in the company's space and AI businesses.
Armed with that information, it becomes clear how hard it will be for Elon Musk to raise the cash he wants at anywhere near SpaceX’s most recent $1.25 trillion valuation. It explains why he’s pushing so hard to juice demand by getting the stock quickly added to the major stock indexes.
Whether the stock soon joins the S&P 500 and other big indexes is critically important, because inclusion in indexes creates tens of billions of dollars in demand from index investors who will be forced to buy SpaceX shares, no matter how they feel about its business or valuation.
I am watching with an extra degree of curiosity to see how the indexes handle this, because a few years back I helped pick the stocks that made up the Dow Jones Industrial Average. It was the strangest part of my job running the finance coverage at The Wall Street Journal.
At the time, the Journal’s editor-in-chief was responsible for picking the 30 stocks in the Dow. In practice, one of the paper’s top finance editors did all of the work. That was me. (The Journal’s editor no longer picks the stocks.)
The goal was to have a mix of companies that reflected the U.S. economy, which had shifted away from the industrial sector and toward finance, tech and healthcare. Tech was easy: We added Microsoft and Intel in 1999. And the big pharma companies were obvious.
We also added financial companies like banks and insurers. Those industries had been growing fast, thanks to a boom period with relatively low interest rates and a soaring housing market. That meant a ton of mortgages, which helped spur a flood of financial innovations, including the collateralized debt obligations and credit default swaps that turned toxic in the downturn.
In early 2008, we added Bank of America. Months later, the bottom fell out of the financial system. Over the next year and a half, we pulled Citigroup and AIG (and General Motors) out of the index after Washington bailed them all out.
How does this relate to SpaceX? Because it’s a reminder of how things can go wrong on what you think are certain bets. Back in the years immediately preceding 2008, few believed the housing boom would end. But it did, leading to a nationwide decline in home prices that had never happened before. Investors who thought mortgages were safe and lucrative investments lost big. Among those investors were the banks and insurers we had put into the Dow.
Fast-forward to the present. While tech has struggled this year, few people can imagine a yearslong sell-off that wipes out trillions in investments. But valuations are relatively high, debt levels have risen and the sector has lashed itself to the booming yet unproven market for AI.
Musk has also tied SpaceX to the AI boom with his promise to put data centers into space, as my colleague Theo Wayt
has reported
. That is presumably Musk’s justification for the valuation of SpaceX’s successful but slow-growing rocket company, its lagging,
cash-burning AI company
and its
solidly performing satellite
internet service.
If the initial public offerings of SpaceX, and later Anthropic and OpenAI, come in below investors’ lofty expectations, the damage could be widespread. The tech sector accounts for about a third of the U.S. stock market’s value, as well as a large portion of its gains over the past three years.
SpaceX won’t join the Dow anytime soon. That doesn’t matter much because very little money tracks that benchmark. The index that matters is the S&P 500—roughly $20 trillion in both index funds and investments are benchmarked against it.
S&P has pretty strict requirements about letting companies in. SpaceX will pass most of them but could get tripped up by requirements that companies be profitable and rules about their float, meaning the portion of a company’s shares that trade on the market.
If SpaceX does get into the index at a $1.25 trillion valuation, the stock would land in the top 10 of the market at current prices, right behind Tesla and making up roughly 2% of the index. That would spur some $400 billion of buying. Knowing those forced buyers were waiting in the wings, investors could invest with confidence their shares would go up.
Such perceived safety in what are actually risky assets has often been the cause of financial crises.
Here’s where problems could crop up. My colleague Cory’s reporting shows that SpaceX lost $5 billion last year and that $11.4 billion of its $18.7 billion in revenue came from its satellite internet company, Starlink. The space and AI businesses burned $17 billion in cash, while Starlink generated about $3 billion.
Yet SpaceX is valued at 266 times last year’s earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization. Every company above it in size is valued at a fraction of that amount except for Tesla, which comes in at 119 times.
If Musk’s dreams of robotaxis, robots and data centers in space don’t come true, millions of investors could be stuck holding two expensive stocks without much to justify their prices. It won’t be another financial crisis, but it will cause real losses in the markets.

标题: China’s Probe of Meta’s Manus Purchase Sends Startups Scrambling
内容:
China’s AI startup founders are jittery. The Chinese government’s investigation of
Manus’
sale to
Meta Platforms
has cast a shadow over a popular exit path for startup founders. And it has caused some startups to consider moving out of China or to shift some operations to Singapore.
While some AI startups are happy to stay in China, relying on venture capital funding from China and Asia, those with ambitions to sell their products globally and to raise money from U.S. venture capitalists are taking a different view.
“If you want to build AI products for markets outside China now, you will have to think even more carefully about which markets to target, how to structure your business and whether to raise money in Chinese yuan or U.S. dollars,” said
Hank Yuan
, a co-founder of a new Shenzhen-based startup that is trying to build an AI agent for the global market.
“All the AI startup founders I know are paying attention to Manus,” he added.
Most founders declined to be named for fear of attracting scrutiny from either the Chinese or the U.S. government. But others agreed that the Manus investigation was weighing on founders’ minds.
“Originally, we thought we had many options for exits. But now the takeaway from Manus is: If your startup is acquired by other companies, don’t get acquired by U.S. companies,” a co-founder of a Beijing-based AI video startup said. “If you are acquired by Alibaba or Tencent, that’s fine,” he said.
Manus, a general-purpose AI agent, was developed by
Butterfly Effect
, a company initially founded in Wuhan and Beijing. After the product went viral in March last year, Butterfly Effect raised $75 million from
Benchmark
, and shortly afterwards, the startup decided to move its headquarters and core engineering teams to Singapore, The Information
reported
previously.
By July, the makeover was complete, the China offices were shut down and the company scrapped its earlier plan to launch a product in China. In late December, Meta acquired Manus.
The acquisition sparked an investigation by China’s
Ministry of Commerce
, into whether the acquisition was consistent with “with the relevant laws and regulations on export controls, technology import and export, and outbound investment.”
A person close to Manus said the company had consulted with lawyers before signing the dotted line with Meta, and determined the transaction did not need Chinese government approval. However, the company didn’t give Chinese regulators a heads-up before announcing the deal—a move usually would win some goodwill with the government—for fear that regulators could intervene, the person said.
Last month, the Chinese government imposed an exit ban on Manus’ co-founders
Red Xiao
and
Peak Ji
when they were visiting China, forbidding them from leaving the country’s borders until the investigation is complete, according to a person with direct knowledge of the situation. The Financial Times first reported the exit ban.
The investigation and exit ban have had a chilling effect on Chinese startups. One Chinese founder in the Bay Area recently gave up on the plan to hire engineers in China for the startup he’s building in the U.S. Instead, he decided to hire engineers in Singapore.
“Having a team in Singapore costs more, and the quality isn’t as good as having a China team. But I still don’t want to build a China team. It’s too risky,” he said.
A Shenzhen-based startup founder paused his fundraising plan recently, even after having secured commitments from a few Chinese venture firms. Alarmed by the Manus probe, the founder is switching gears and focusing on relocating his staff from China to Singapore and the U.S..
The startup has no corporate presence in China, and is registered in the U.S., with most of the staff working in Shenzhen under contracts with an outsourcing firm. But recent developments made that arrangement untenable. “The Manus situation is a wakeup call. I can’t drag my feet anymore thinking the current structure is going to work,” he said.
There are others who decide to stay and fully embrace their China identity. The Beijing-based AI video startup is planning to change its corporate structure so that it could start raising funding in Chinese yuan from the next round. The startup has an offshore structure and only raised funding in the dollar—a common practice by Chinese startups that want to target customers beyond China.
A Hangzhou-based founder also decided to stay put. While that might mean his company can never raise money from American VCs, “you can still raise money from investors in China, Singapore and the Middle East,” he says.
Here’s what else is going on…
People on the Move
Anthropic
said Tuesday that the CEO of pharmaceutical company
Vas Narasimhan
is joining its board
, highlighting Anthropic’s increased focus on healthcare and life sciences.
Aparna Ramani
,
Meta’s
vice president of engineering for AI infrastructure,
announced her departure
Tuesday in an internal memo. She reported to Meta’s chief AI officer,
Alexandr Wang
.
Mike Krieger
, an
Instagram
cofounder who previously was
Anthropic’s
Chief Product Officer and now leads the company’s “Labs” team,
stepped down
from
Figma’s
board of directors on Tuesday. The move came shortly after The Information broke the
news
that Anthropic is working on an AI design tool which could compete with Figma’s offerings
Broadcom
CEO
Hock Tan
will not stand for reelection to Meta’s board, CNBC reported.
Policy Watch
Anthropic
opposes Illinois AI bill
SB 3444
, Wired reported. The bill would shield the biggest AI companies from liability for “critical harms” caused by their models as long as they did not intentionally or recklessly cause such an incident.
OpenAI
supports the bill.
Separately, residents of
Festus
, a small town in Missouri, voted out all four incumbent city council members after they approved the construction of a data center near the town, Politico reported.
Deals and Debuts
See The Information’s
Generative AI Database
for an exclusive list of private companies and their investors.
Anthropic
has received offers from investors that would value the company as high as $800 billion, Business Insider and Bloomberg reported.
Fluidstack
, which builds specialized data centers for AI companies, is in talks to raise a $1 billion round at an $18 billion valuation led by
Jane Street
, according to a Bloomberg report.
nEye.ai
, which makes optical circuit switch chips for AI data centers, raised $80 million in a Series C funding round led by
Sutter Hill Ventures
.
Mintlify
, which uses AI to automatically generate documentation for software companies, raised $45 million at a $500 million valuation in a Series B funding round led by
Andreessen Horowitz
and
Salesforce Ventures
.
Bluefish
, which helps brands track how their products are described by AI shopping tools, raised $43 million in a Series B funding round led by
Threshold Ventures
and
NEA
.
Synera
, which provides AI agents for industrial manufacturing, raised $40 million in a Series B funding round led by
Revaia
.
Gitar
, a startup that uses agents to secure code, raised $9 million in funding led by
Venrock
.
Anthropic
is preparing its next flagship model,
Claude Opus 4.7
, along with a new AI-powered tool for designing websites and presentations, which could be released as soon as this week, The Information
reported
.
OpenAI
gave some cybersecurity companies access to
GPT-5.4-Cyber
, a version of OpenAI’s model with fewer safety guardrails that was customized to excel at tasks related to cybersecurity.
The technology team at the
U.S. Treasury Department
is seeking access to
Anthropic’s Mythos
model as soon as this week, Bloomberg reported. Mythos has been shared with a select group of companies to work on cybersecurity. Meanwhile, Anthropic cofounder
Jack Clark
confirmed that the company had briefed the Trump administration about its new Mythos model in an interview this week.
Salesforce
has launched a tool
that lets its customers connect other companies’ AI agents with their Salesforce data, an executive said on LinkedIn. The tool, known as a hosted
Model Context Protocol
server, shows how Salesforce is finding a way to generate revenue from customers using outside AI agents, following similar moves by rivals such as
ServiceNow
and
Workday
.
Amazon
released
Bio Discovery
, which provides biology researchers access to AI models for drug discovery. Researchers can send their drug candidates to labs to be synthesized.
Google
announced
skills
, which are repeatable prompts that
Chrome
users can give
Gemini
using keyboard shortcuts.
Nvidia
released
Ising
, a pair of AI models for quantum computing.

标题: OpenAI Plans New Pricing for ChatGPT Ads, Explores Other Upgrades
内容:
OpenAI plans to start pricing some ChatGPT ads based on whether people click on the ads rather than just how many people see them, an agency executive who spoke with OpenAI employees and works with ChatGPT advertisers said.
At the same time, OpenAI has also indicated it plans to introduce ads aimed at getting people to take a specific action, like making a purchase or downloading an app, the agency executive said, though it hasn’t put a firm timeline on when that could happen. The discussions reflect OpenAI’s efforts to make ChatGPT more appealing for marketers as it chases its ambitious growth targets and looks to challenge Meta Platforms and Google.
OpenAI, which started showing ads in ChatGPT in early February, has so far only provided early advertisers with aggregated data such as impressions, clicks and spend. Digital ad giants Meta and Google, by contrast, provide more information on who sees ads and whether they lead to purchases.
Marketers have said it’s
difficult to tell if
ChatGPT’s early ads are working and likely need stronger tracking and measurement tools from OpenAI before they’ll move beyond small, experimental ad spending. Offering pricing on a per-click basis would let advertisers pay when users engage with an ad, while conversion-oriented ad campaigns could let them optimize spending toward actions like purchases.
“A lot of clients who would have the budget to want to test on a new surface are holding back and are tentative because they don’t think they’re going to be able to get the measurement that they want out of it,” said Ben Kahan, head of the programmatic practice at marketing agency Brainlabs.
OpenAI began testing ads in early February for logged-in U.S. users of the free and lowest-priced version of ChatGPT, and so far the rollout has been slow. The company has extended the pilot period for its earliest advertisers past the end of March because it hasn’t shown ads often enough to exhaust the initial spending commitments, two people managing ChatGPT ads on behalf of advertisers said.
